Longer-term implications: if courts can review the prerogative power to prorogue, it becomes harder to maintain other prerogative powers (e.g. deployment of armed forces; appointment of ministers; exercise of mercy) as genuinely non-justiciable. The GCHQ subject-matter test is now less predictive — courts may find justiciable questions embedded within what were thought to be non-justiciable exercises. The Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022 (repealing the FTPA 2011) restored the dissolution prerogative to the Crown — but Miller No 2's principle that prerogative cannot be used to frustrate Parliament applies equally to dissolution.

The deepest unresolved tension: Miller No 2 has not been legislatively reversed, but its constitutional legitimacy continues to be contested. Whether it represents a principled application of existing doctrine or an unprecedented judicial assertion of power over the executive-Parliament relationship remains an open and live debate — and one whose resolution may depend less on legal analysis than on the political culture of judicial restraint.
Judicial review of prerogative powers.summary_line: MILLER NO 2 · PROROGATION · JUSTICIABILITY · ELLIOTT · YOUNG · FINNIS · EKINS · DISSOLUTION ACT 2022 · BOUNDARY

Relationship with statute.front: What is the principle from R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995]?
Relationship with statute.answer:
R v Secretary of State ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995] — the Home Secretary used prerogative power to introduce a new criminal injuries compensation scheme, knowing that a statutory scheme under the Criminal Justice Act 1988 had been enacted but not yet brought into force.

Held (HL, 3–2): The Home Secretary could not use prerogative to introduce a scheme that would make it impossible to implement the statutory scheme Parliament had passed. Using prerogative in a manner inconsistent with statutory intention was unlawful.

Key principle: the executive cannot use prerogative powers to frustrate the will of Parliament or render statutory provisions nugatory.

Note also: the prerogative does revive if a statute is repealed — De Keyser remains good law. FBU adds the further rule that prerogative cannot be used to undermine an enacted statute.

⚠️ EXAM TRAP: Distinguish De Keyser (prerogative in abeyance where statute IS in force) from FBU (prerogative cannot frustrate a statute even where not yet in force). Students who stop at De Keyser will miss FBU on these facts. See also FLK1-CAL-TRAP-009.
Relationship with statute.summary_line: FBU 1995 · PREROGATIVE CANNOT FRUSTRATE STATUTE · HOME SECRETARY · CRIMINAL INJURIES · PARLIAMENT WILL

Relationship with statute.front: Attorney General v De Keyser's Royal Hotel Ltd [1920]
Relationship with statute.issue: Whether the Crown could use prerogative powers to requisition a hotel during wartime without paying compensation, where statutory powers covering the same ground existed.
Relationship with statute.rule: Where Parliament has legislated in an area previously covered by prerogative, the prerogative is placed in abeyance for as long as the statute operates. The Crown cannot choose to exercise the prerogative in preference to the statute in order to avoid obligations (such as compensation) that the statute imposes.
Relationship with statute.application: The Crown requisitioned the hotel under prerogative powers, arguing it owed no compensation. The Defence of the Realm Acts covered the same power of requisition but required compensation. The HL held that once Parliament had addressed the field by statute, the prerogative was displaced. The Crown was required to pay compensation under the statute.
Relationship with statute.conclusion: The prerogative was in abeyance; the statute applied; compensation was due. The case establishes the fundamental principle that statute trumps prerogative in the same field. The prerogative is not destroyed — if the statute were repealed, it would revive. But while the statute operates, the Crown must use statutory rather than prerogative powers.
Relationship with statute.summary_line: DE KEYSER · PREROGATIVE IN ABEYANCE · STATUTE PREVAILS · COMPENSATION · CANNOT CHOOSE PREROGATIVE · REVIVES ON REPEAL

Relationship with statute.front: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995]
Relationship with statute.issue: Whether the Home Secretary could use prerogative power to introduce a new criminal injuries compensation scheme where Parliament had enacted a statutory scheme that had not yet been brought into force.
Relationship with statute.rule: The executive cannot use prerogative powers to frustrate the will of Parliament or to render a statutory provision nugatory. Where Parliament has enacted a statutory scheme, the executive must not use prerogative to introduce an alternative scheme that makes it impossible or unnecessary to implement the statutory one.
Relationship with statute.application: The Criminal Justice Act 1988 established a statutory compensation scheme, but had not been brought into force. The Home Secretary used prerogative to introduce a new tariff-based scheme, knowing this would make the statutory scheme redundant. The majority held this was unlawful — the prerogative could not be used to effectively repeal a statute by executive action.
Relationship with statute.conclusion: The prerogative instruction was unlawful (3-2 majority). The case adds a further limit on prerogative beyond De Keyser: not only must the executive use statute rather than prerogative where both exist, but it cannot use prerogative to undermine a statute that exists but is not yet in force. Note: the prerogative does revive if a statute is expressly repealed.
Relationship with statute.summary_line: FBU · PREROGATIVE CANNOT FRUSTRATE STATUTE · CRIMINAL INJURIES · STATUTORY SCHEME · EXECUTIVE CANNOT REPEAL BY PREROGATIVE

Relationship with statute.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

The Emergency Powers Act 1996 gave the Crown sweeping requisition powers during national emergencies. The Act is repealed in 2010 with no replacement legislation. In 2025, a national emergency arises. A government lawyer advises: 'The prerogative power to protect the realm is gone — the 1996 Act replaced it, and now that Act has been repealed, there is no power left. We need new primary legislation urgently.'

A second lawyer disagrees and says primary legislation is not needed. Who is right?
Relationship with statute.issue: The Trap: The first lawyer's reasoning follows a logical sequence: statute replaced prerogative (De Keyser); statute has been repealed; therefore the prerogative went with the statute and nothing remains. This feels correct because students learn that statute 'prevails over' the prerogative and assume that means the prerogative is extinguished. The red herring is the word 'replaced' — which implies permanent displacement.
Relationship with statute.rule: The Reality: The second lawyer is correct. Under the principle in Attorney General v De Keyser's Royal Hotel [1920], when Parliament legislates in an area previously covered by prerogative, the prerogative falls into abeyance — it is suspended while the statute operates, but it is not abolished. When the statute is repealed and not replaced, the prerogative revives. The Crown can therefore exercise its prerogative to protect the realm without new primary legislation.
Relationship with statute.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The trap is conflating abeyance with abolition. De Keyser itself makes clear that the prerogative is 'placed in abeyance' — it 'cannot be exercised while the statute operates.' The corollary, confirmed by R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995], is that if the statute is repealed, the prerogative revives. The 1996 Act did not destroy the prerogative; it only suspended it. Repeal of the Act removes the suspension.
Relationship with statute.conclusion: The Key Rule: Statute places prerogative in abeyance — it does not abolish it. When the statute is repealed, the prerogative revives. Abeyance ≠ abolition.
Relationship with statute.summary_line: ABEYANCE NOT ABOLITION · DE KEYSER · REVIVES ON REPEAL · FBU · PREROGATIVE SURVIVES REPEAL

Relationship with statute.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Parliament passes the Emergency Criminal Injuries Compensation Act 2024. The Act receives Royal Assent but no commencement order is made — it has not been brought into force.

The Home Secretary decides to use prerogative power to introduce a new tariff-based criminal injuries compensation scheme. Her legal adviser tells her: 'The 2024 Act is not in force — it is as if it does not exist. The prerogative is available to fill the gap. The De Keyser principle applies only where a statute is actually operating, not where it has been enacted but not commenced.' The legal adviser is wrong.
Relationship with statute.issue: The Trap: The legal adviser's reasoning is based on a careful reading of De Keyser: the prerogative falls into abeyance only while 'the statute operates.' An Act that has not been commenced cannot be said to be 'operating.' Therefore, the reasoning goes, the prerogative is available in the interim gap before commencement. The red herring is the technically correct observation about commencement — which is accurate as far as De Keyser goes, but misses the further principle that De Keyser's rule was extended in FBU.
Relationship with statute.rule: The Reality: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995] goes beyond De Keyser. The HL held that the executive cannot use prerogative power to introduce a scheme that makes it impossible or unnecessary to bring a statutory scheme into force. This applies even where the statute has been enacted but not yet commenced. Using prerogative to make the 2024 Act redundant — even before it is brought into force — is unlawful.
Relationship with statute.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The legal adviser has correctly described the De Keyser rule but has failed to identify FBU as the controlling authority on these facts. FBU was decided on materially identical facts: the Criminal Justice Act 1988 had been enacted but not commenced; the Home Secretary used prerogative to introduce a rival scheme. The HL held this was unlawful — Parliament's decision to legislate represents its will, and the executive cannot use prerogative to frustrate that will even before commencement. The 2024 Act's enactment (not commencement) is what creates the constraint.
Relationship with statute.conclusion: The Key Rule: FBU extends De Keyser. The executive cannot use prerogative to make a statute redundant even before commencement. Parliament's enactment of a scheme is the relevant act — not its coming into force. De Keyser governs active statutes; FBU governs enacted but uncommenced statutes.
Relationship with statute.summary_line: FBU EXTENDS DE KEYSER · ENACTED NOT COMMENCED · PREROGATIVE CANNOT FRUSTRATE · ENACTMENT IS THE CONSTRAINT

Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.front: The government wishes to requisition private land for use as a military training ground. A senior official argues the Crown can do this under prerogative powers without paying compensation, pointing to the broad common law powers of the Crown in matters of national defence. The landowner's solicitor disagrees. Advise.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.answer:
The landowner's solicitor is likely correct. The key question is whether Parliament has legislated in this area, displacing the prerogative under the De Keyser principle.

The prerogative power to defend the realm and requisition property for military purposes exists in principle at common law. However, where Parliament has legislated to cover the same ground, the prerogative falls into abeyance and the statutory regime applies instead: Attorney General v De Keyser's Royal Hotel [1920].

In De Keyser, the Crown argued it could requisition a hotel under prerogative to avoid paying statutory compensation. The House of Lords held that the Defence of the Realm Acts covered the same power and required compensation — the prerogative was in abeyance and the Crown could not choose the prerogative route to escape its statutory obligations.

In practice, requisition of land for military purposes is now governed by statute (e.g. Land Powers (Defence) Act 1958 and associated legislation), which provides for compensation. The Crown cannot bypass this statutory scheme by reverting to prerogative.

Conclusion: the Crown must use the statutory power and pay compensation as required by the relevant legislation. Prerogative cannot be used where statute covers the field.

⚠️ Abeyance ≠ abolition. If the statute were expressly repealed, the prerogative would revive.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.summary_line: DE KEYSER · PREROGATIVE IN ABEYANCE · STATUTE PREVAILS · COMPENSATION · CANNOT CHOOSE PREROGATIVE · ABEYANCE NOT ABOLITION

Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.front: Parliament passes the Criminal Injuries (Statutory Scheme) Act 2024, creating a detailed compensation framework for victims of violent crime, but the Act has not yet been brought into force by commencement order. The Home Secretary, using prerogative powers, introduces a new tariff-based scheme that would make it financially unnecessary to ever bring the statutory scheme into force. Advise on the lawfulness of this.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.answer:
The Home Secretary's use of prerogative is unlawful, applying the principle from R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995].

In FBU, the Home Secretary used prerogative to introduce a new criminal injuries scheme, knowing this would make the statutory scheme under the Criminal Justice Act 1988 redundant, even though that scheme had been enacted but not brought into force. The House of Lords held (3-2) that this was an unlawful use of prerogative — the executive cannot use prerogative to frustrate the will of Parliament or render a statutory provision nugatory.

Applying FBU to these facts: the 2024 Act has been enacted by Parliament, representing Parliament's will that a statutory compensation scheme should exist. The Home Secretary's prerogative scheme is designed to make the statutory scheme redundant. This is precisely the conduct condemned in FBU — using prerogative to effectively repeal a statute by executive action.

The Home Secretary should instead bring the statutory scheme into force, or seek to amend it through further primary legislation if dissatisfied with its terms.

Conclusion: the prerogative scheme is unlawful. The correct remedy would be a mandatory order requiring the Home Secretary to consider bringing the statutory scheme into force.

⚠️ Distinguish from De Keyser (statute already in force, prerogative in abeyance): FBU goes further — even a statute not yet in force limits prerogative action that would make it unworkable.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.summary_line: FBU · PREROGATIVE CANNOT FRUSTRATE STATUTE · CRIMINAL INJURIES · NOT YET IN FORCE · DE KEYSER DISTINGUISHED

Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.front: The Foreign Secretary signs an international trade treaty with a third country using prerogative powers. The treaty, once in force, will require the UK to permit the import of goods that are currently banned under domestic consumer protection legislation. A pressure group challenges the treaty. Advise.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.answer:
Two distinct issues arise: (1) the legality of treaty-making by prerogative; and (2) the relationship between the treaty and the domestic ban.

(1) Treaty-making is a prerogative power. The Crown retains the power to enter into international treaties using prerogative powers — this is a recognised and non-justiciable prerogative in the GCHQ sense (Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]). The courts will not generally review the merits of treaty-making decisions — they are non-justiciable by subject matter.

(2) However, treaties do not automatically become part of domestic law in the UK's dualist system. For the treaty's terms to have domestic legal effect — including permitting the import of currently banned goods — Parliament must incorporate the treaty by statute. Until Parliament legislates, the domestic consumer protection legislation remains in force and the import ban applies.

The principle from R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the EU [2017] (Miller No 1) reinforces this: the prerogative cannot be used to alter domestic statutory rights. Parliament created the consumer protection ban; only Parliament can remove it.

Conclusion: the treaty is validly made but has no domestic legal effect without parliamentary implementation. The pressure group cannot prevent the treaty being signed, but Parliament's approval would be needed before the ban could lawfully be lifted.

⚠️ Treaty-making = non-justiciable prerogative. But treaty implementation requires statute — dualist system.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.summary_line: TREATY PREROGATIVE · NON-JUSTICIABLE · DUALIST · NO DOMESTIC EFFECT · PARLIAMENT REQUIRED · MILLER NO 1

Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.front: Parliament passes the Emergency Powers Act 2025, which provides detailed powers for the government to respond to national emergencies, including powers of requisition and detention. A public health emergency arises. The Prime Minister argues that because the Act is inadequate for the scale of the crisis, the government should use the residual prerogative powers of the Crown — including the prerogative to protect the realm — rather than the Act. Advise.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.answer:
The Prime Minister's position is almost certainly unlawful. Under the De Keyser principle, once Parliament has legislated to cover the same ground as a prerogative power, the prerogative is placed in abeyance — the Crown cannot choose to exercise the prerogative in preference to the statute.

Here, Parliament has specifically enacted the Emergency Powers Act 2025 to govern the government's response to national emergencies. The government cannot argue the statute is 'inadequate' as a basis for reverting to prerogative — the proper response to an inadequate statute is to seek amendment through Parliament (legislative process), not to bypass Parliament's chosen framework.

The FBU principle goes further still: even if the government believes the prerogative would allow more effective action, using it to circumvent the statutory scheme would frustrate Parliament's will — that is unlawful: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995].

The only possible exception would be if the statute entirely failed to address a particular type of situation, leaving a gap that the prerogative could fill. But on these facts, the Act covers emergency responses generally — it is claimed to be inadequate in degree, not inapplicable in kind. That is insufficient to revive the prerogative.

Conclusion: the government must use the 2025 Act. If additional powers are needed, emergency primary legislation through Parliament is the constitutional route.

⚠️ This scenario is a classic exam trap — students may think 'national emergency' triggers special prerogative powers. It does not where statute covers the field.
Prerogative scope / abeyance / FBU.summary_line: DE KEYSER · ABEYANCE · STATUTE COVERS FIELD · FBU · CANNOT CHOOSE PREROGATIVE · AMEND BY LEGISLATION

JR of prerogative / justiciability.front: The Foreign Secretary exercises prerogative powers to refuse to renew a passport for a UK citizen, Amir, who is suspected (but not convicted) of involvement in terrorism. Amir seeks judicial review. The government argues the exercise of passport powers is non-justiciable. Advise.
JR of prerogative / justiciability.answer:
Amir has reasonable prospects of judicial review being granted, notwithstanding the government's argument.

Following the GCHQ case [1985], prerogative powers are in principle subject to judicial review. The source of the power (prerogative) is irrelevant to reviewability — what matters is the subject matter and the nature of the power exercised. The three grounds (illegality, irrationality, procedural impropriety) are available.

Non-justiciable prerogatives: the GCHQ case identified certain prerogative powers as non-justiciable — treaty-making, national security decisions, deployment of armed forces, dissolution of Parliament, mercy. These are non-justiciable because courts lack the institutional competence to evaluate them.

Passport decisions fall into an intermediate category. The prerogative power over passports is justiciable as to procedural fairness and proportionality, even if courts give deference to the Foreign Secretary's assessment of security risk: R v Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs ex p Everett [1989] confirmed passport decisions are judicially reviewable.

Grounds available to Amir:
— Procedural impropriety: was he given notice and an opportunity to respond to the grounds for refusal?
— Irrationality/proportionality: is the refusal proportionate, particularly given Art 8 ECHR (private and family life) and freedom of movement implications?
— Illegality: was the power used for a proper purpose?

Conclusion: the government's non-justiciability argument will fail. The court will review the decision, though it may afford the Foreign Secretary some deference on the national security assessment.

⚠️ Not all prerogative powers affecting individuals are non-justiciable. Courts distinguish by subject matter.
JR of prerogative / justiciability.summary_line: PREROGATIVE REVIEWABLE · GCHQ · SUBJECT MATTER TEST · PASSPORT · EVERETT · PROCEDURAL FAIRNESS · DEFERENCE

JR of prerogative / justiciability.front: The Prime Minister advises the Monarch to prorogue Parliament for eight weeks immediately before a critical vote on proposed legislation that would limit executive powers. A backbench MP seeks judicial review of the prorogation advice. Advise on the prospects of success, applying Miller No 2.
JR of prerogative / justiciability.answer:
The backbench MP has strong prospects of success, applying R (Miller) v Prime Minister [2019] (Miller No 2).

In Miller No 2, the Supreme Court unanimously held that: (1) the prerogative power to prorogue Parliament is justiciable — courts can review its lawfulness; (2) the power has legal limits — it cannot be used to frustrate Parliament's ability to carry out its constitutional functions without reasonable justification; (3) a five-week prorogation in the Brexit context, with no justification offered, was unlawful and void.

Applying Miller No 2 to these facts: the prorogation is for eight weeks (longer than the five weeks condemned in Miller No 2) and is timed specifically to prevent a parliamentary vote on legislation that would constrain the executive. This is a direct attempt to frustrate Parliament's constitutional function of scrutinising and legislating — precisely what Miller No 2 prohibits.

The government would need to provide reasonable justification for the prorogation. On these facts, the timing and purpose suggest no legitimate justification — the dominant purpose appears to be preventing the vote.

Remedies: if unlawful, the prorogation would be void and of no effect. The court could make a declaration to that effect, and the Speaker could recall Parliament (as happened after Miller No 2).

⚠️ Miller No 2 pushed the boundary of justiciability significantly — prorogation was previously thought to be a core, non-justiciable prerogative. The subject matter test from GCHQ must now be applied carefully.
JR of prerogative / justiciability.summary_line: MILLER NO 2 · PROROGATION JUSTICIABLE · CONSTITUTIONAL FUNCTIONS · VOID · NO JUSTIFICATION · EIGHT WEEKS

JR of prerogative / justiciability.front: The Prime Minister issues a prerogative instruction that all civil servants with access to sensitive intelligence files must submit to enhanced vetting. A civil servants' union argues this breaches their members' Art 8 ECHR rights (private life) and the absence of consultation is procedurally unfair. The government argues national security makes the instruction non-justiciable. Advise.
JR of prerogative / justiciability.answer:
The government's non-justiciability argument is likely to succeed on the national security element, but the position is more nuanced than a blanket exclusion.

GCHQ framework: the GCHQ case [1985] confirmed that prerogative powers relating to national security are non-justiciable — courts will not second-guess the executive's assessment of what national security requires. In GCHQ itself, the employees' legitimate expectation of consultation was overridden by national security — the court accepted the government's evidence that even the fact of consultation would create a security risk.

However, GCHQ does not mean all aspects of the instruction are immune. Courts will:
(1) Review whether the power exists — is there a prerogative power to direct civil service vetting? (Likely yes, under the general prerogative over civil service management.)
(2) Potentially review proportionality under the HRA: Art 8 is engaged by intrusive vetting. Under s.6 HRA, the government as a public authority must not act incompatibly with Art 8. A court would ask whether the vetting measures are proportionate to the security aim — but will give significant deference to the government on the national security assessment: Bank Mellat [2013].

On consultation: following GCHQ, if the government provides credible evidence that prior consultation itself poses a security risk, the procedural impropriety argument will fail — national security can override the duty to consult.

Conclusion: the instruction is likely lawful, but the court will conduct at least a limited review to ensure the power exists and was not exercised irrationally or in bad faith.

⚠️ National security ≠ automatic immunity. Courts still review existence of power and whether national security grounds are genuine.
JR of prerogative / justiciability.summary_line: NATIONAL SECURITY · GCHQ · NON-JUSTICIABLE · ART 8 · DEFERENCE · PROPORTIONALITY · LIMITED REVIEW

Convention rights and their scope.front: Which ECHR rights are incorporated by the Human Rights Act 1998? Distinguish absolute, limited, and qualified rights.
Convention rights and their scope.answer:
The HRA 1998 incorporates the rights in the European Convention on Human Rights as set out in Schedule 1. Key rights:

Absolute rights — cannot be derogated from or limited by competing interests:
— Art 3: prohibition on torture, inhuman or degrading treatment
— Art 4(1): prohibition on slavery
— Art 7: no punishment without law

Limited rights — can be restricted in specific defined circumstances:
— Art 5: right to liberty (specific grounds for lawful detention)
— Art 6: right to a fair trial (some limitations possible)

Qualified rights — can be interfered with where the interference is prescribed by law, pursues a legitimate aim, and is necessary in a democratic society (proportionality):
— Art 8: private and family life
— Art 9: freedom of thought, conscience and religion
— Art 10: freedom of expression
— Art 11: freedom of assembly and association

⚠️ For qualified rights, all three conditions (prescribed by law / legitimate aim / necessary / proportionate) must be satisfied — failing any one renders the interference unlawful.
Convention rights and their scope.summary_line: HRA 1998 · ABSOLUTE · LIMITED · QUALIFIED · ARTS 3 8 10 11 · PROPORTIONALITY TEST

Convention rights and their scope.front: What is the proportionality test used when assessing interference with qualified Convention rights?
Convention rights and their scope.answer:
When a public authority interferes with a qualified right (Arts 8–11 ECHR), the court applies a structured proportionality analysis:

(1) Is the interference prescribed by law? — the legal basis must be sufficiently clear, accessible, and foreseeable.
(2) Does it pursue a legitimate aim? — the ECHR specifies permitted aims for each right (e.g. Art 8(2): national security, public safety, prevention of disorder or crime, protection of health or morals, rights of others).
(3) Is it necessary in a democratic society? — the measure must correspond to a pressing social need and be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued.
(4) Is it proportionate? — the least restrictive means to achieve the aim; a fair balance between individual rights and community interests.

Key case: Bank Mellat v HM Treasury (No 2) [2013] — Supreme Court set out a four-stage proportionality framework now applied in judicial review.

⚠️ Proportionality is a more intensive standard than Wednesbury unreasonableness and applies whenever a Convention right is engaged.
Convention rights and their scope.summary_line: PROPORTIONALITY · PRESCRIBED BY LAW · LEGITIMATE AIM · NECESSARY · BANK MELLAT · QUALIFIED RIGHTS

Convention rights and their scope.front: What is the 'margin of appreciation' doctrine, and does it apply in domestic HRA proceedings?
Convention rights and their scope.answer:
The margin of appreciation is a doctrine of the European Court of Human Rights by which Strasbourg allows member states a degree of latitude in how they implement Convention rights, recognising that national authorities are better placed to assess local conditions and values.

It does NOT apply in domestic HRA proceedings. The leading domestic authority is R v DPP ex p Kebilene [2000] (Lord Hope); confirmed by Lord Bingham in R (SB) v Governors of Denbigh High School [2006]. UK courts must themselves determine whether Convention rights have been respected — they cannot defer to a domestic 'margin of appreciation.'

However, domestic courts do apply an analogous concept — the 'discretionary area of judgment' or 'deference' — in certain contexts where the executive or legislature has particular expertise, democratic accountability, or institutional competence:
— National security decisions
— Sensitive policy choices with resource implications
— Matters of 'social and ethical controversy' where Parliament has considered the issue

Key case: R v Director of Public Prosecutions ex p Kebilene [2000] — Lord Hope noted that domestic courts should give a 'deference' to the judgment of democratic institutions in appropriate cases.

⚠️ Margin of appreciation = Strasbourg doctrine. Deference/discretionary area of judgment = domestic equivalent. Do not conflate them.
Convention rights and their scope.summary_line: MARGIN OF APPRECIATION · ECTHR ONLY · NOT DOMESTIC · DEFERENCE · DENBIGH · KEBILENE · DISCRETIONARY AREA

Convention rights and their scope.front: What does Article 6 ECHR guarantee, and what are its key requirements in civil and criminal proceedings?
Convention rights and their scope.answer:
Article 6 ECHR guarantees the right to a fair trial. Its scope differs between civil and criminal proceedings.

Art 6(1) — applies to both civil and criminal:
— The right to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time
— By an independent and impartial tribunal established by law
— With judgment pronounced publicly

Art 6(2) — criminal only:
— Presumption of innocence until proven guilty according to law

Art 6(3) — criminal only (minimum rights):
— To be informed promptly of the charge in a language understood
— Adequate time and facilities to prepare a defence
— To defend in person or with legal assistance (and free legal aid where interests of justice require)
— To examine witnesses and call witnesses on equal terms
— Free interpreter if cannot understand the language

Key domestic cases:
— R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Anderson [2002]: mandatory life tariff-setting by the Home Secretary breached Art 6 — a minister is not an independent tribunal.
— Brown v Stott [2003]: not every departure from strict rules of evidence breaches Art 6; the right must be assessed as a whole.

⚠️ Art 6 is a limited right — it can be subject to implied limitations provided they pursue a legitimate aim and do not impair the very essence of the right.
Convention rights and their scope.summary_line: ART 6 ECHR · FAIR TRIAL · INDEPENDENT TRIBUNAL · CIVIL AND CRIMINAL · PRESUMPTION OF INNOCENCE · ANDERSON

Convention rights and their scope.front: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Anderson [2002]
Convention rights and their scope.issue: Whether the Home Secretary's power to set the tariff (minimum term) for mandatory life sentence prisoners was compatible with Art 6 ECHR (right to a fair trial by an independent and impartial tribunal).
Convention rights and their scope.rule: The determination of the minimum term a prisoner must serve before being considered for release is the determination of a criminal penalty. Such a decision must be made by an independent and impartial tribunal under Art 6 ECHR. A government minister is not an independent tribunal and cannot lawfully perform this function.
Convention rights and their scope.application: The Home Secretary exercised a statutory power to fix the tariff for mandatory life prisoners, often departing upward from the judicial recommendation. The HL held this was incompatible with Art 6 — the minister was not independent of the executive. The sentencing function had to be exercised by a court.
Convention rights and their scope.conclusion: A declaration of incompatibility under s.4 was made. Parliament responded by transferring the tariff-setting power to trial judges via the Criminal Justice Act 2003. A clear illustration of the HRA's structural effect on the relationship between the executive and the judiciary in criminal sentencing.
Convention rights and their scope.summary_line: ANDERSON · ART 6 · FAIR TRIAL · INDEPENDENT TRIBUNAL · HOME SECRETARY · TARIFF · S.4 DECLARATION

Convention rights and their scope.front: Going deeper: Could Parliament repeal the Human Rights Act 1998 without replacement — and what constitutional protection for rights would remain?
Convention rights and their scope.answer:
The question of HRA repeal has moved from the academic to the political following Conservative proposals for a British Bill of Rights (2022 Bill of Rights Bill, withdrawn 2023) and continuing government pressure. It tests what rights protection is available as a matter of common law and constitutional principle, independent of the HRA.

What repeal would remove: (1) the s.3 obligation to read legislation compatibly; (2) the s.4 declaration of incompatibility mechanism; (3) the s.6 duty on public authorities; (4) the s.7/8 victim test and damages regime. Parliament could legislate incompatibly with Convention rights without any domestic remedy.

What would remain: (1) The ECHR itself — the UK remains a party to the Convention and individuals could still petition Strasbourg (though enforcement is diplomatic, not legal); (2) Common law rights protection — English courts have developed a body of common law constitutional rights (access to justice: Raymond v Honey [1983]; legal professional privilege: R v Derby Magistrates' Court ex p B [1996]; privacy to some extent: Campbell v MGN [2004]) which do not depend on the HRA; (3) The principle of legality (Simms) — Parliament needs express words to remove fundamental rights, irrespective of the HRA; (4) Proportionality in JR where Convention-adjacent rights are engaged.

Academic assessment: Conor Gearty (On Fantasy Island, 2016) argues the HRA has not significantly changed the culture of human rights in the UK — repeal would matter less than its proponents fear, because courts would adapt common law to fill the gap. Francesca Klug (A Magna Carta for All Humanity, 2015) argues the HRA's dialogue mechanism (s.3/s.4) is constitutionally unique and its loss would fundamentally weaken rights protection. Aileen Kavanagh ('The Lure and the Limits of Dialogue' [2016] UTLJ) takes a middle position: the dialogue model is valuable but the courts have developed it beyond its original remit.

The unresolved tension: without the HRA, common law rights protection is less systematic, less visible, and accessible only through creative judicial development — which depends on the willingness of individual judges and cannot be relied upon for consistent protection across all rights.
Convention rights and their scope.summary_line: HRA REPEAL · COMMON LAW RIGHTS · PRINCIPLE OF LEGALITY · GEARTY · KLUG · KAVANAGH · ECHR STRASBOURG · DIALOGUE

Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.front: What does s.3 HRA 1998 require, and what are its limits?
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.answer:
Section 3 HRA 1998 provides: 'So far as it is possible to do so, primary legislation and subordinate legislation must be read and given effect in a way which is compatible with Convention rights.'

Scope: s.3 applies to all legislation, past and future. It is a strong interpretive obligation — courts must strive to read legislation compatibly even if that requires departing from the natural meaning of the words.

Key case: R v A (No 2) [2001] — HL read down s.41 Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999 to allow admission of complainant's sexual history where necessary to prevent an unfair trial (Art 6).

Limits (Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]):
— S.3 cannot be used to make an order that is incompatible with a fundamental feature of the legislation.
— It cannot require a decision that the court has no power to make.
— It cannot be used to legislate — if compatible reading is not possible, the remedy is s.4 (declaration of incompatibility).

⚠️ The limit is: does the proposed reading go against the grain of the legislation? If yes, s.3 is unavailable.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.summary_line: S.3 HRA · POSSIBLE TO DO SO · READ COMPATIBLY · R v A · GHAIDAN · GRAIN OF LEGISLATION · LIMITS

Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.front: When should a court use s.3 HRA 1998 rather than s.4? What factors determine the choice?
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.answer:
S.3 (compatible interpretation) is the primary remedy — courts must attempt it before resorting to s.4 (declaration of incompatibility).

Factors favouring s.3:
— A compatible reading is linguistically possible and does not contradict the core purpose of the legislation.
— The incompatibility is at the margins (e.g. a specific provision rather than the whole scheme).
— Reading down produces a result Parliament could reasonably have intended.

Factors requiring s.4 instead:
— Compatible interpretation would go against 'the grain' of the legislation: Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004].
— It would require the court to make choices that are properly for Parliament (e.g. selecting between policy options).
— It would require adding words that create a new positive obligation rather than limiting an existing one.
— Re S (Minors) [2002] — HL refused to use s.3 to rewrite a scheme of care orders; s.4 issued instead.

⚠️ S.4 does not affect validity — it is a signal to Parliament to amend. S.3 actually changes the legal effect of the legislation.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.summary_line: S.3 FIRST · S.4 IF IMPOSSIBLE · GRAIN TEST · GHAIDAN · RE S · COURT CANNOT LEGISLATE

Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.front: What did Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004] establish about the scope and limits of s.3 HRA 1998?
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.answer:
Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]:

Facts: Paragraph 2(2) of Schedule 1 to the Rent Act 1977 gave the surviving spouse of a statutory tenant the right to succeed to a tenancy. Mr Mendoza was the same-sex partner of a deceased statutory tenant and sought the same protection.

Held (HL): Using s.3 HRA 1998, the words 'as his or her wife or husband' could be read to include same-sex partners — compatible with Art 14 (non-discrimination) and Art 8 (family life).

Key principles from Lord Nicholls and Lord Millett:
(1) S.3 permits a court to read words in a way that is contrary to their express or implied meaning — not just a modest correction.
(2) Limit: s.3 cannot be used where the proposed reading would go against the grain of the legislation — i.e. the proposed meaning would be incompatible with the fundamental features of the legislative scheme.
(3) S.3 must not make decisions Parliament has not authorised — if the reading changes the effect of the legislation in a way Parliament clearly did not intend, s.4 is the correct route.

⚠️ Ghaidan defines the outer limits of s.3 — it can be powerful but not unlimited. The test is whether the proposed reading is consistent with the fundamental features of the statute.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.summary_line: GHAIDAN 2004 · S.3 HRA · READING COMPATIBLY · SAME-SEX PARTNER · GRAIN OF LEGISLATION · LIMITS ON S.3

Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.front: What are the constitutional tensions created by s.3 HRA 1998, and how have courts navigated the boundary between interpretation and legislation?
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.answer:
Section 3 HRA 1998 creates a structural tension between judicial interpretation and parliamentary sovereignty — it requires courts to read legislation compatibly with Convention rights 'so far as it is possible to do so,' but the extent of that obligation is contested.

The tension:
— Too narrow a reading of s.3: courts apply s.4 declarations frequently, leaving incompatible laws in force and pressuring Parliament — but respects democratic authority.
— Too wide a reading of s.3: courts effectively rewrite legislation, usurping Parliament's role — judicially convenient but constitutionally questionable.

How courts have navigated this:
— R v A (No 2) [2001] (Lord Steyn): s.3 is a 'strong' interpretive obligation — courts must adopt a compatible reading even if it requires departure from legislative intent, unless it is impossible.
— Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004] (Lord Nicholls/Lord Millett): the limit is 'going against the grain' — a compatible reading that contradicts the fundamental feature of the legislation crosses the line from interpretation to amendment.
— Re S (Minors) [2002] (Lord Nicholls): s.3 cannot be used to make 'wholesale changes' to the legislative scheme — that is Parliament's role; the court issued a s.4 declaration instead.

Key principle: s.3 permits reading words in a strained or unusual way; it does not permit adding a new scheme or making choices between policy options Parliament has not authorised.

⚠️ Link — s.3 limits (see FLK1-CAL-021 and FLK1-CAL-065): the Ghaidan 'grain of the legislation' test and the R v A strong obligation must be read together.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.summary_line: S.3 TENSION · INTERPRETATION v LEGISLATION · R v A · GHAIDAN · RE S · GRAIN TEST · PARLIAMENTARY ROLE

Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.issue: Whether s.3 HRA 1998 required the Rent Act 1977 to be read so as to give a same-sex surviving partner the same succession rights as a surviving spouse.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.rule: Section 3 HRA 1998 permits courts to read and give effect to legislation in a way that is contrary to its express or implied meaning, provided this does not go against the grain of the legislation or contradict a fundamental feature of the statutory scheme. S.3 is a strong interpretive obligation — more than merely resolving ambiguity.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.application: The Rent Act 1977 gave succession rights to a person living with the tenant 'as his or her wife or husband.' Mr Mendoza had lived with his male partner. The HL read the provision to include same-sex partners, finding this was compatible with the grain of the legislation (which was to protect long-term cohabitants). This avoided a declaration of incompatibility under s.4.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.conclusion: The Rent Act was read compatibly under s.3. The case defines the outer limits of s.3: it allows strained or unusual readings but not readings that contradict the fundamental features of the scheme or that require the court to make choices properly belonging to Parliament. Where s.3 cannot achieve compatibility, s.4 is the only option.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.summary_line: GHAIDAN · S.3 HRA · STRONG OBLIGATION · SAME-SEX PARTNER · GRAIN OF LEGISLATION · LIMITS OF S.3

Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A barrister is advising on a case in which a statutory provision is clearly incompatible with Art 8 ECHR. She tells her client: 'The provision is incompatible, so we will apply to the High Court for a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998. There's no point trying a s.3 argument — the natural meaning is clearly incompatible, and we should go straight to the declaration.'

The judge hearing the application tells the barrister she cannot proceed in this way. The judge is correct.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.issue: The Trap: The barrister's reasoning is that s.3 and s.4 are alternative remedies — if incompatibility is obvious, the more efficient route is a straight s.4 declaration. This feels pragmatic and logical. The trap is treating s.3 and s.4 as genuinely parallel options that counsel can choose between based on efficiency.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.rule: The Reality: Section 3 is not an alternative to s.4 — it is a precondition. Courts must attempt a s.3 compatible interpretation before resorting to a s.4 declaration. A court cannot issue a s.4 declaration without first determining that s.3 compatible interpretation is impossible. The structure of the HRA makes s.4 a last resort, not a first option: Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]; Re S (Minors) [2002].
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The barrister has jumped to s.4 because the natural meaning of the provision is incompatible. But the 'natural meaning' being incompatible is exactly the situation s.3 was designed to address — s.3 requires courts to depart from the natural meaning if a compatible reading is possible. The question is not whether the natural meaning is compatible, but whether any reading is possible that achieves compatibility without going against the grain of the legislation (Ghaidan). Only after a genuine s.3 analysis concludes that no compatible reading is possible may the court make a s.4 declaration.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.conclusion: The Key Rule: S.3 must always be attempted before s.4. A court cannot make a declaration of incompatibility without first finding that compatible interpretation under s.3 is impossible. S.4 is a last resort — not a parallel option.
Section 3 \u2014 interpretation obligati.summary_line: S.3 BEFORE S.4 · S.4 LAST RESORT · GHAIDAN · NATURAL MEANING IRRELEVANT · ATTEMPT S.3 FIRST

Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.front: What is a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998, and what are its effects?
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.answer:
Section 4 HRA 1998 empowers higher courts (Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, High Court) to make a declaration that a provision of primary or subordinate legislation is incompatible with a Convention right.

Effects — critically limited:
(1) A declaration of incompatibility does not affect the validity, continuing operation, or enforceability of the provision: s.4(6)(a).
(2) It is not binding on the parties to the proceedings: s.4(6)(b).
(3) Parliament is under no legal obligation to amend — it is a political pressure, not a legal command.

Practical effect: the Fast Track Procedure under s.10 and Schedule 2 HRA 1998 allows a minister to amend the legislation by remedial order without primary legislation — but this is discretionary.

Rationale: preserves parliamentary sovereignty while creating a structured dialogue between judiciary and Parliament.

⚠️ Trap: a declaration of incompatibility does NOT give the claimant a remedy — they may still lose their case.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.summary_line: S.4 HRA · DECLARATION · NO EFFECT ON VALIDITY · NO REMEDY FOR CLAIMANT · S.10 REMEDIAL ORDER · SOVEREIGNTY

Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.front: Which courts have jurisdiction to make a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998?
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.answer:
Section 4(5) HRA 1998 restricts the power to make declarations of incompatibility to specific courts:

(1) The Supreme Court
(2) The Judicial Committee of the Privy Council
(3) The Court of Appeal (and the Court Martial Appeal Court)
(4) The High Court (including the Administrative Court)
(5) The Court of Session (Scotland) and the High Court of Justiciary (Scotland)

Significance: lower courts — including magistrates' courts, county courts, and tribunals — do NOT have jurisdiction to make declarations of incompatibility. They must apply the legislation as it stands (and attempt s.3 interpretation), or refer the issue to a higher court if possible.

Procedural requirement: the Crown must be given notice and the right to intervene where a court is considering making a declaration of incompatibility (s.5 HRA 1998), since a finding of incompatibility directly engages parliamentary and governmental decision-making.

⚠️ A declaration of incompatibility is not automatically made wherever incompatibility exists — the court must first exhaust the s.3 compatible interpretation route. S.4 is the last resort.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.summary_line: S.4 HRA · WHICH COURTS · SUPREME COURT · COURT OF APPEAL · HIGH COURT · NOT MAGISTRATES · S.5 CROWN NOTICE

Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Maria is a prisoner whose Art 5 ECHR rights have been violated by a statutory provision requiring automatic segregation without any hearing. She brings proceedings in the High Court. The court finds that s.3 HRA 1998 cannot rescue the provision — a compatible reading is impossible — and makes a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998.

Maria's solicitor tells her: 'The declaration means the provision is unlawful, so you're entitled to a remedy — damages and release from segregation.' The prison governor disagrees. The governor is right.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.issue: The Trap: The solicitor's reasoning feels correct. A court has found a breach of Maria's Convention rights. Courts grant remedies for unlawful acts. Therefore Maria gets a remedy. This reasoning — that a declaration of incompatibility equals a finding of unlawfulness for which the individual is compensated — is the trap most candidates fall into.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.rule: The Reality: A declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998 does not give Maria any remedy. Section 4(6) HRA 1998 is explicit: (a) a declaration does not affect the validity, continuing operation, or enforcement of the provision; and (b) it is not binding on the parties to the proceedings. The provision remains law. Maria remains subject to it. The prison governor is entitled to continue applying it.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The red herring is the word 'declaration' — it sounds like a judicial finding of unlawfulness that creates rights. But the HRA 1998 deliberately preserved parliamentary sovereignty. A s.4 declaration is a signal to Parliament, not a remedy for the individual. The court is telling Parliament the provision is incompatible — Parliament may choose to amend (using the s.10 fast-track remedial order procedure) or may not. Until Parliament acts, the provision stands and Maria has no remedy from the declaration itself. To get a remedy, Maria would need to establish a separate cause of action — e.g. a private law claim, or a s.7/8 HRA claim if there is an executive act (not just the statutory provision) that violated her rights.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.conclusion: The Key Rule: A s.4 declaration of incompatibility gives the claimant NO remedy. It does not affect validity, is not binding on the parties, and does not require Parliament to act. Parliamentary sovereignty is preserved throughout.
Section 4 \u2014 declarations of incompa.summary_line: S.4 DECLARATION · NO REMEDY · VALIDITY UNAFFECTED · NOT BINDING · PARLIAMENTARY SOVEREIGNTY · S.4(6)

Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.front: What is a 'public authority' under s.6 HRA 1998? Distinguish core and hybrid public authorities.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.answer:
Section 6(1) HRA 1998: it is unlawful for a public authority to act in a way incompatible with a Convention right.

Section 6(3) identifies two categories:

(1) Core public authorities — bodies whose nature is wholly public (e.g. central government departments, police, local authorities, courts and tribunals). They must comply with Convention rights in all they do, including private acts.

(2) Hybrid (functional) public authorities — private bodies exercising functions of a public nature: s.6(3)(b). They must comply with Convention rights when performing public functions but NOT when performing private functions: s.6(5).

Key case: Aston Cantlow and Wilmcote with Billesley Parochial Church Council v Wallbank [2003] — the test is functional, not institutional. Factors: statutory basis; public funding; public accountability; whether taking the place of government.

⚠️ YL v Birmingham City Council [2007] — private care home operator not a public authority even when placing local authority-funded residents. Now partially reversed by Health and Social Care Act 2008 for care homes.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.summary_line: S.6 HRA · PUBLIC AUTHORITY · CORE · HYBRID · FUNCTIONAL TEST · ASTON CANTLOW · YL

Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.front: What is the 'public function' test under s.6(3)(b) HRA 1998? What factors does the court consider?
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.answer:
A body exercises a 'function of a public nature' under s.6(3)(b) HRA 1998 if the function is one that would otherwise be performed by a government body.

Factors identified in Aston Cantlow [2003] and R (Heather) v Leonard Cheshire Foundation [2002]:
(1) Whether the function is publicly funded or subsidised
(2) Whether the body is subject to public regulation or statutory control
(3) Whether it acts in the place of central or local government
(4) Whether there is a statutory regime under which it operates
(5) Whether the relationship with service users is governed by contract (suggesting private character)

Key contrast:
— Leonard Cheshire (charity running care homes under contract with local authority): not a public authority — contractual relationship was private.
— YL v Birmingham CC [2007]: reinforced — commercial provider, contractual relationship, private law character.

⚠️ The function-by-function analysis means the same body can be a public authority for some acts and not for others — each act must be assessed individually.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.summary_line: S.6(3)(b) · PUBLIC FUNCTION TEST · ASTON CANTLOW · CHESHIRE · CONTRACTUAL = PRIVATE · ACT BY ACT

Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.front: What is a 'hybrid' public authority under s.6 HRA 1998, and what is the effect of s.6(5)?
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.answer:
A hybrid public authority (also called a functional public authority) under s.6(3)(b) HRA 1998 is a private body that exercises some functions of a public nature alongside private functions.

Section 6(5): a hybrid public authority is not required to comply with Convention rights in respect of acts that are private in nature. The obligation under s.6(1) applies only to public functions.

Consequence: a single body may be a public authority for some purposes and not for others, depending on the nature of the specific act in question.

Example: a private company running a prison under contract with the Government — public functions (detaining, disciplining prisoners) attract HRA obligations; private functions (internal HR decisions, commercial contracts) do not.

Key cases:
— Aston Cantlow [2003]: the parochial church council was not a core public authority and in the particular context was not exercising a public function.
— YL v Birmingham CC [2007]: private care home operator not exercising public functions even when performing state-contracted services — reversed by statute for care homes only.

⚠️ The act-by-act analysis is critical: each individual action must be classified, not the body as a whole.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.summary_line: HYBRID PUBLIC AUTHORITY · S.6(5) · PRIVATE ACTS EXCLUDED · ACT BY ACT · ASTON CANTLOW · YL

Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.front: Aston Cantlow and Wilmcote with Billesley Parochial Church Council v Wallbank [2003]
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.issue: Whether a parochial church council (PCC) was a 'public authority' under s.6 HRA 1998, such that enforcement of chancel repair liability against lay rectors engaged Convention rights.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.rule: The test for whether a body is a public authority under s.6(3)(b) HRA 1998 is functional, not institutional. A body exercises a 'function of a public nature' if it is performing a role that government would otherwise perform. Relevant factors include: statutory underpinning; public accountability; public funding; whether it is taking the place of central or local government.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.application: The PCC sought to enforce chancel repair liability against the Wallbanks (lay rectors) — an ancient common law obligation. The HL held the PCC was not a core public authority (it was not governmental in nature) and was not exercising a public function in enforcing chancel repair — this was a private law obligation between private parties, notwithstanding the Church of England's established status.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.conclusion: The PCC was not a public authority for these purposes; the HRA did not apply. The case establishes the functional test for hybrid public authorities and confirms that the established status of the Church of England does not automatically make church bodies public authorities. The key question is always whether the specific function being performed is governmental.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.summary_line: ASTON CANTLOW · S.6 HRA · FUNCTIONAL TEST · NOT PUBLIC AUTHORITY · CHANCEL REPAIR · HYBRID · PRIVATE LAW

Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

CareFirst Ltd is a private company that runs several care homes. Some residents are funded by the local authority; others are self-funding. CareFirst also employs around 300 staff, manages its own HR and disciplinary procedures, and enters into commercial contracts with suppliers.

The local authority-funded resident, Mr. Osei, brings an HRA claim arguing CareFirst breached his Art 8 rights in relation to his care. CareFirst's lawyer argues: 'CareFirst has been confirmed as a public authority for the purposes of providing publicly-funded residential care. Therefore, HRA obligations apply to everything CareFirst does.'

Mr. Osei's solicitor should be cautious about accepting this argument.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.issue: The Trap: CareFirst's lawyer has correctly stated that CareFirst is a public authority for certain functions. The trap is the conclusion — that because CareFirst is a public authority for some purposes, HRA obligations apply to everything it does. This reasoning would make CareFirst a core public authority, which it is not. The red herring is the confirmed public authority status, which applies only to specific functions.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.rule: The Reality: CareFirst is a hybrid (functional) public authority under s.6(3)(b) HRA 1998 — not a core public authority. Under s.6(5), the HRA obligation applies only to CareFirst's acts that are public in nature. When CareFirst performs private acts (managing staff, entering commercial contracts, internal HR decisions), it is not acting as a public authority and the HRA does not apply to those acts.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The distinction between core and hybrid public authorities is exactly this: core authorities (like local councils) must comply with Convention rights in everything they do, including private acts. Hybrid authorities must comply only when performing public functions. CareFirst is hybrid — confirmed as a public authority only when providing publicly-funded residential care (following the statutory fix in s.145 Health and Social Care Act 2008 reversing YL v Birmingham CC [2007]). CareFirst's employment decisions, procurement contracts, and commercial arrangements are private acts. The HRA does not apply to them. Mr Osei's solicitor is right to be cautious: the public authority status is function-specific, not institution-wide.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.conclusion: The Key Rule: Hybrid public authorities under s.6(3)(b) HRA are bound by Convention rights ONLY for their public functions — not for private acts (s.6(5)). Being a public authority for one function does not make a body a public authority for all functions.
Section 6 \u2014 public authorities.summary_line: HYBRID PUBLIC AUTHORITY · S.6(5) · PUBLIC FUNCTIONS ONLY · PRIVATE ACTS EXCLUDED · NOT CORE · YL · S.145 HSCA

Section 7 and 8 \u2014 standing and reme.front: Who has standing to bring a claim under s.7 HRA 1998?
Section 7 and 8 \u2014 standing and reme.answer:
Section 7(1) HRA 1998: a person may bring proceedings against a public authority under s.6(1) only if they are (or would be) a 'victim' of the unlawful act.

The victim test mirrors the test under Art 34 ECHR for bringing a claim to the European Court of Human Rights:
— Direct victim: the person directly affected by the act or omission.
— Potential victim: a person under a real risk of being directly affected (e.g. a law is not yet applied to them but may be).
— Indirect victim: close family members where the primary victim is deceased or incapacitated.

⚠️ The victim test is narrower than the 'sufficient interest' test for ordinary judicial review under CPR 54. Pressure groups and campaign organisations generally cannot bring HRA claims as victims — they can only bring ordinary judicial review. Contrast: R v Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs ex p World Development Movement Ltd [1995] — standing in ordinary JR is broader.
Section 7 and 8 \u2014 standing and reme.summary_line: S.7 HRA · VICTIM TEST · ART 34 ECHR · DIRECT POTENTIAL INDIRECT · NARROWER THAN SUFFICIENT INTEREST

Section 7 and 8 \u2014 standing and reme.front: What remedies are available under s.8 HRA 1998, and what limits apply?
Section 7 and 8 \u2014 standing and reme.answer:
Section 8(1) HRA 1998: where a court finds an act of a public authority was unlawful under s.6(1), it may grant such relief or remedy within its powers as it considers just and appropriate.

Available remedies:
— Declaration of incompatibility (for primary legislation under s.4)
— Quashing/setting aside the unlawful act
— Injunction
— Damages — but only where: (a) necessary to afford just satisfaction; (b) the court has the power to award damages; and (c) taking account of all the circumstances (s.8(3))

Limits on damages:
— Courts must take into account principles of the ECtHR when awarding damages: s.8(4).
— ECtHR damages are often lower than domestic tort awards.
— Damages are not automatic — just satisfaction is the standard.

⚠️ A declaration of incompatibility under s.4 does not give the claimant a remedy — they may still need to bring a separate damages claim if one is available.
Section 7 and 8 \u2014 standing and reme.summary_line: S.8 HRA · JUST AND APPROPRIATE · DAMAGES · JUST SATISFACTION · ECTHR PRINCIPLES · NO AUTOMATIC AWARD

Derogations and reservations.front: What are derogations and reservations under the ECHR and HRA 1998?
Derogations and reservations.answer:
Derogation (Art 15 ECHR): A state may derogate from certain Convention obligations in time of war or other public emergency threatening the life of the nation, but only to the extent strictly required by the exigencies of the situation, and not from absolute rights (Arts 2, 3, 4(1), 7).

The UK derogated following 9/11 — detained foreign nationals without trial under Anti-Terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001. Held unlawful: A v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2004] (Belmarsh case) — the measures were disproportionate and discriminatory (applied to foreign nationals only). Derogation quashed.

Reservation: A state may opt out of specific articles at the time of ratification. The UK has a reservation in respect of Art 2 Protocol 1 (right to education) — the UK accepted it only insofar as it is compatible with efficient instruction and training, and the avoidance of unreasonable public expenditure.

⚠️ Arts 3, 4(1), and 7 cannot be derogated from under any circumstances. Art 2 is also non-derogable EXCEPT in respect of deaths resulting from lawful acts of war (Art 15(2) ECHR) — this is the one qualification on Art 2's non-derogable status.
Derogations and reservations.summary_line: DEROGATION · ART 15 ECHR · EMERGENCY · ABSOLUTE RIGHTS EXCLUDED · BELMARSH · RESERVATION

Derogations and reservations.front: What did A v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2004] (the Belmarsh case) decide?
Derogations and reservations.answer:
A v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2004] (HL) — commonly called the Belmarsh case — is the most significant domestic HRA decision on the relationship between security powers and human rights.

Facts: Following 9/11, the Anti-Terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001 allowed indefinite detention without trial of non-UK nationals suspected of terrorism, who could not be deported. The UK had derogated from Art 5 ECHR (right to liberty) under Art 15 to permit this.

Held (8–1, HL):
(1) The derogation was invalid — the measures did not rationally address the threat. The threat (international terrorism) was not limited to foreign nationals; detaining only non-UK suspects was disproportionate and discriminatory (Art 14 read with Art 5).
(2) A declaration of incompatibility was issued under s.4 HRA 1998.
(3) Lord Hoffmann (dissenting on the emergency point, concurring on the result): the real threat to the life of the nation was not terrorism but the Government's own response to it — the rule of law was itself at stake.

Significance: (1) demonstrated the HRA's teeth even against security legislation; (2) showed that a derogation can be judicially scrutinised and struck down as disproportionate; (3) Parliament responded by replacing detention with control orders (Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005).

⚠️ Link — Derogations and reservations (see FLK1-CAL-028): the Belmarsh case is the leading domestic application of Art 15 ECHR — the UK's derogation was found inadequate.
Derogations and reservations.summary_line: BELMARSH 2004 · ART 5 · DEROGATION INVALID · DISPROPORTIONATE · S.4 DECLARATION · RULE OF LAW

Derogations and reservations.front: A v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2004]
Derogations and reservations.issue: Whether the indefinite detention of foreign nationals suspected of terrorism under the Anti-Terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001 was compatible with the ECHR, and whether the UK's derogation from Art 5 was valid.
Derogations and reservations.rule: A derogation from Art 5 ECHR under Art 15 requires a public emergency threatening the life of the nation. Any derogating measures must be strictly required by the exigencies of the situation. Measures that are disproportionate or discriminatory (applying only to foreign nationals when the threat is not so limited) will not satisfy Art 15. Courts can scrutinise the validity of a derogation.
Derogations and reservations.application: The House of Lords held (8-1): (1) there was a public emergency threatening the life of the nation (this was accepted by the majority, though Lord Hoffmann dissented powerfully); (2) but the measures were disproportionate and discriminatory — they applied only to non-UK nationals when the terrorist threat was not so limited; UK nationals with identical profiles were free. The derogation was therefore invalid.
Derogations and reservations.conclusion: A declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998 was issued. Parliament responded with the Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005, replacing detention with control orders. The case is the high-water mark of the HRA's impact on security legislation and demonstrates that courts will scrutinise derogations, not simply accept executive assertions of necessity.
Derogations and reservations.summary_line: BELMARSH · ART 5 · DEROGATION INVALID · DISPROPORTIONATE · DISCRIMINATORY · S.4 DECLARATION · RULE OF LAW

Derogations and reservations.front: Going deeper: Does the ECHR's derogation mechanism (Art 15) provide adequate protection against erosion of rights during national emergencies — and what does the Belmarsh litigation reveal about its limitations?
Derogations and reservations.answer:
The Art 15 ECHR derogation mechanism is designed to permit states to respond to genuine emergencies while maintaining minimum rights standards. Its adequacy has been significantly tested by post-9/11 counter-terrorism legislation.

The mechanism and its limits: Art 15 permits derogation from most Convention rights in time of 'war or other public emergency threatening the life of the nation', subject to the measures being 'strictly required by the exigencies of the situation.' Non-derogable rights (Arts 3, 4(1), 7, and Art 2 except in lawful war) provide an absolute floor. The mechanism requires notification to the Council of Europe Secretary-General and is subject to ECtHR review.

The Belmarsh litigation (A v Secretary of State [2004]) revealed three structural weaknesses: (1) the 'life of the nation' threshold is largely self-certified by governments — the majority in Belmarsh accepted the UK faced such an emergency, though Lord Hoffmann (dissenting memorably) argued that the real threat to the nation was the government's own response; (2) the 'strictly required' test provides limited protection where governments frame measures carefully — the Belmarsh majority found the measures failed on discrimination grounds, not on the necessity analysis; (3) the remedy (a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA) carries no legal compulsion — Parliament is free not to respond.

Scholarly assessment: Helen Fenwick (Civil Liberties and Human Rights, 4th edn) argues the UK's post-Belmarsh response (control orders, then TPIMs — Terrorism Prevention and Investigation Measures Act 2011) perpetuated the same structural problem in subtler form: quasi-administrative restrictions with limited procedural safeguards. David Bonner (Executive Measures, Terrorism and National Security, 2007) identifies a persistent 'derogation creep' — states exploit emergencies to normalise exceptional measures that outlast the emergency.

The comparative dimension: the US approach (no equivalent derogation mechanism; judicial review through due process clauses; habeas corpus) produced a different but not obviously superior outcome in the post-9/11 litigation (Boumediene v Bush [2008] SC reversed the suspension of habeas corpus). France's state of emergency law (2015-17) was criticised by the Venice Commission for excessive executive discretion. The tension between effective emergency response and rights protection appears irreducible across all systems.
Derogations and reservations.summary_line: ART 15 · DEROGATION · BELMARSH · FENWICK · BONNER · TPIMS · HOFFMANN · STRICTLY REQUIRED · COMPARATIVE

S.3 / s.4 choice.front: A statute passed in 1985 provides that asylum seekers 'shall not be eligible for legal aid in any proceedings.' A court is asked whether this provision is compatible with Art 6 ECHR (right of access to a court). The government argues the court should simply apply the statute as written. Advise on the court's options under the HRA 1998.
S.3 / s.4 choice.answer:
The court has two options under the HRA 1998 — s.3 (compatible interpretation) and s.4 (declaration of incompatibility) — and must consider s.3 first.

S.3 HRA 1998 requires the court to read and give effect to the 1985 provision 'so far as it is possible to do so' in a way compatible with Convention rights. Art 6 ECHR protects the right of access to a court — the ECtHR has held that denial of legal aid in civil proceedings may breach Art 6 where the case is sufficiently complex that the applicant cannot effectively participate without legal assistance.

Can s.3 help? The court should consider whether the phrase 'shall not be eligible for legal aid in any proceedings' can be read compatibly — for example, whether 'any proceedings' could be read as excluding proceedings where the denial of legal aid would breach Art 6. This would be a strained but potentially possible reading.

If compatible interpretation is impossible — if the provision expressly and unambiguously excludes legal aid in all circumstances, and reading it otherwise would contradict the fundamental feature of the legislative scheme — then the court cannot use s.3. It must instead consider s.4.

S.4: the court (if at High Court level or above) may make a declaration that the provision is incompatible with Art 6. This does not affect the validity of the provision — it remains in force and the asylum seeker will not receive legal aid unless Parliament responds. The fast-track procedure under s.10 allows a minister to amend the legislation by remedial order.

⚠️ S.3 is always tried first. S.4 is a last resort — and it gives the claimant no direct remedy.
S.3 / s.4 choice.summary_line: S.3 FIRST · S.4 LAST RESORT · ART 6 · COMPATIBLE READING · DECLARATION NO REMEDY · S.10 REMEDIAL ORDER

S.3 / s.4 choice.front: A statute provides that in immigration appeals, 'the tribunal shall have no power to consider evidence not available at the date of the original decision.' An immigration judge believes this prevents her from considering evidence showing that the appellant will be tortured if deported, in breach of Art 3 ECHR (absolute prohibition on torture). Applying s.3 HRA 1998, advise.
S.3 / s.4 choice.answer:
The immigration judge should apply s.3 HRA 1998 to read the provision compatibly with Art 3.

Art 3 ECHR is an absolute right — it cannot be derogated from or limited by any competing interest. If deportation would expose the appellant to a real risk of torture, returning them is unlawful regardless of any procedural rule. The state cannot use a procedural provision to facilitate a breach of an absolute right.

S.3 analysis: The statutory phrase 'no power to consider evidence not available at the date of the original decision' could potentially be read as subject to an implied qualification — that it does not prevent consideration of evidence where failure to do so would require the tribunal to facilitate a breach of Art 3. This strained but plausible reading avoids the incompatibility.

Applying Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004] and R v A (No 2) [2001]: s.3 allows courts to depart from the natural meaning of words where necessary to achieve Convention compatibility, provided the reading does not go against the grain of the legislation. The fundamental purpose of an immigration appeal system is to ensure correct and lawful decisions — allowing consideration of risk of torture is consistent with that purpose, not contrary to it.

Conclusion: the judge should use s.3 to read the provision as not precluding consideration of evidence going to Art 3 risk. If that is genuinely impossible (the provision is too clearly expressed), a s.4 declaration should be made — though deportation could not proceed in the interim without risking a breach of the absolute prohibition.

⚠️ Absolute rights create the strongest case for s.3 — a court will strain very hard to avoid facilitating a breach of Art 3.
S.3 / s.4 choice.summary_line: S.3 · ART 3 ABSOLUTE · TORTURE · STRAINED READING · R v A · GHAIDAN · GRAIN TEST

S.3 / s.4 choice.front: Parliament enacts the Housing Allocation Act 2025, which creates a detailed scheme for allocating social housing, including a provision that 'no account shall be taken of the applicant's family circumstances in determining priority.' A single mother with three children argues this breaches Art 8 ECHR. The court finds a compatible reading under s.3 is not possible. What happens next?
S.3 / s.4 choice.answer:
If a compatible reading under s.3 is not possible, the court must consider s.4 HRA 1998.

S.4 declaration of incompatibility: a higher court (Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, or High Court) may make a declaration that the provision is incompatible with the Convention right — here, Art 8 ECHR (right to respect for private and family life). Art 8 is a qualified right: interference must be prescribed by law, pursue a legitimate aim, and be necessary/proportionate.

Critical effects of a s.4 declaration:
(1) It does NOT affect the validity, continuing operation, or enforcement of the provision: s.4(6)(a) HRA 1998. The Housing Allocation Act continues to operate as enacted.
(2) It is NOT binding on the parties: s.4(6)(b). The single mother does not win her case and does not get priority allocation.
(3) Parliament is under no legal obligation to amend — but will face political pressure to do so.
(4) A minister may use the fast-track remedial order procedure under s.10 and Schedule 2 to amend the legislation without a full Act of Parliament.

Parliamentary sovereignty is preserved throughout: the declaration is a signal in a dialogue between courts and Parliament, not a judicial override.

Conclusion: the court makes the declaration. The single mother's case is likely dismissed. Parliament may or may not respond.

⚠️ EXAM TRAP: students often assume a declaration of incompatibility gives the claimant a remedy. It does not.
S.3 / s.4 choice.summary_line: S.4 DECLARATION · NO EFFECT ON VALIDITY · NO REMEDY FOR CLAIMANT · S.10 REMEDIAL ORDER · SOVEREIGNTY PRESERVED

S.3 / s.4 choice.front: A statute provides that in proceedings relating to a national security certificate, 'the Special Immigration Appeals Commission shall reach its decision on the basis of the Secretary of State's certificate alone, without receiving or considering any other evidence.' A claimant argues this is incompatible with Art 6 ECHR. The court accepts incompatibility exists. Can s.3 assist?
S.3 / s.4 choice.answer:
S.3 cannot assist here. This is a case where the provision is so clearly and deliberately expressed that any compatible reading would go against the grain of the legislation — the core situation where s.3 is unavailable: Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]; Re S (Minors) [2002].

The provision expressly and comprehensively excludes the tribunal from receiving or considering evidence beyond the Secretary of State's certificate. Parliament clearly intended to create a closed procedure for national security cases. Reading in an exception — for example, 'unless necessary for Art 6 compliance' — would not be a linguistic exercise but wholesale judicial rewriting of a deliberate legislative scheme.

Applying the Ghaidan test: a compatible reading would go against the fundamental feature of the provision (which is to create a closed, certificate-only process). The grain of the legislation is the exclusion of other evidence — s.3 cannot reverse that.

This is the paradigm case for s.4. The court should make a declaration of incompatibility.
