Parliament would then face a choice: defend the incompatibility (politically difficult given the absolute terms of Art 6) or introduce a Special Advocates procedure or similar mechanism (as was eventually done in other national security contexts).

Conclusion: s.3 is unavailable. A s.4 declaration of incompatibility should be made. The provision remains in force; the claimant does not receive other evidence; Parliament must decide how to respond.

⚠️ Where Parliament has clearly and deliberately enacted an incompatible scheme, s.3 cannot rewrite it — even for fundamental rights. S.4 is the only judicial tool.
S.3 / s.4 choice.summary_line: S.3 UNAVAILABLE · GRAIN OF LEGISLATION · GHAIDAN · RE S · S.4 DECLARATION · DELIBERATE INCOMPATIBILITY

S.6 public authorities.front: Identify whether each of the following is a public authority under s.6 HRA 1998, giving brief reasons: (a) a local authority housing department; (b) a private company running a prison under contract with the Ministry of Justice; (c) a trade union.
S.6 public authorities.answer:
(a) Local authority housing department: a core public authority under s.6(3)(a) HRA 1998. Local authorities are governmental bodies — they are wholly public in nature. Everything they do (including private acts) must be compatible with Convention rights. No further analysis required.

(b) Private company running a prison under contract: a hybrid (functional) public authority under s.6(3)(b), at least for the public functions it performs. Running a prison — detaining, disciplining, and managing prisoners — is a function that would otherwise be performed by the state (the Prison Service). The functional test from Aston Cantlow [2003] is satisfied: the company takes the place of central government in respect of these functions.

However, under s.6(5), the company is only bound by the HRA in respect of its public functions, not its private acts. Its internal HR decisions, procurement contracts, and commercial arrangements are private acts outside the HRA.

(c) Trade union: not a public authority under s.6. Trade unions are private bodies representing members' interests. They are not governmental in nature and do not exercise functions that the state would otherwise perform. Neither core nor hybrid — the HRA does not apply to them as public authorities (though they are subject to ECHR rights indirectly through courts' obligation to act compatibly under s.6(3)(a) — courts are public authorities).

⚠️ The act-by-act analysis for hybrid bodies is critical — the same company may be a public authority for some acts and not for others.
S.6 public authorities.summary_line: PUBLIC AUTHORITY · CORE · HYBRID · S.6(3)(b) · FUNCTIONAL TEST · ASTON CANTLOW · PRIVATE ACTS EXCLUDED

S.6 public authorities.front: Greenfields Ltd is a private care home operator. It runs a care home in which 30 residents are funded by the local authority under adult social care contracts, and 10 residents are self-funding. A self-funding resident, Mrs Patel, claims that Greenfields has breached her Art 8 rights by restricting her from receiving visitors. Can she bring a claim under s.6 HRA 1998?
S.6 public authorities.answer:
Mrs Patel faces difficulty bringing a claim under s.6 HRA 1998, applying the reasoning in YL v Birmingham City Council [2007].

In YL, the House of Lords held (3-2) that a private care home operator running a home under contract with a local authority was not exercising a function of a public nature under s.6(3)(b). The majority reasoned that the relationship between the care home and the resident was essentially contractual and private in character — the care home was a commercial provider, not stepping into the shoes of the state.

Mrs Patel is a self-funding resident — her relationship with Greenfields is purely contractual, with no local authority involvement at all. This makes her case even weaker than the local authority-funded residents in YL. On the YL analysis, Greenfields is not exercising a public function in respect of her placement.

Note however: Parliament responded to YL by enacting s.145 Health and Social Care Act 2008, which deems care home providers to be exercising functions of a public nature when providing care to local authority-funded residents. This statutory fix applies only to publicly-funded placements. Mrs Patel, as a self-funder, falls outside s.145.

Conclusion: Mrs Patel cannot bring a HRA s.6 claim against Greenfields. Her remedies (if any) are in private law — negligence, contract, or the Care Quality Commission complaints regime.

⚠️ YL self-funder = no public authority. The s.145 fix is for publicly-funded residents only.
S.6 public authorities.summary_line: YL · PRIVATE CARE HOME · NOT PUBLIC AUTHORITY · SELF-FUNDER · S.145 HSCA 2008 · PUBLICLY FUNDED ONLY

S.6 public authorities.front: The Church of England carries out two functions: (a) it grants planning permissions for developments on its own church land under a faculty jurisdiction (an ecclesiastical legal system); and (b) it operates a chain of Church of England primary schools, which receive state funding and follow the national curriculum. In respect of which functions, if either, does Art 8 ECHR apply?
S.6 public authorities.answer:
The answer differs between the two functions, applying the functional test from Aston Cantlow v Wallbank [2003].

(a) Faculty jurisdiction over church land: this is an internal ecclesiastical function with no governmental character. The Church of England, though established, is not a governmental body. Granting faculties (permissions to alter church buildings) is not a function that the state would otherwise perform — it is a uniquely ecclesiastical function. On the Aston Cantlow analysis, this is a private function. Art 8 HRA does not apply.

(b) Church of England primary schools receiving state funding: this is a stronger case for public authority status. The relevant factors from Aston Cantlow:
— Statutory underpinning: yes — schools operate under the Education Acts
— Public funding: yes — state-funded
— Public accountability: yes — subject to Ofsted inspection and the national curriculum
— Performing a function that the state would otherwise perform: yes — providing state-funded education

A Church of England school receiving state funding and following the national curriculum is very likely a hybrid public authority when performing its educational functions. In respect of those functions, Art 8 (and other Convention rights) apply.

Conclusion: (a) no HRA obligations; (b) HRA obligations in respect of educational functions.

⚠️ The same institution can be a public authority for some functions and not others — apply the functional test act by act.
S.6 public authorities.summary_line: FUNCTIONAL TEST · ASTON CANTLOW · ECCLESIASTICAL PRIVATE · SCHOOL PUBLIC FUNCTION · STATE FUNDED · ACT BY ACT

S.6 public authorities.front: DataCorp Ltd is a private company contracted by the Home Office to manage the UK's national identity database, including processing all requests for access to personal data held on the database. DataCorp refuses to provide a data subject, Finn, with access to his own records, citing internal efficiency reasons. Finn argues this breaches Art 8. Advise.
S.6 public authorities.answer:
Finn has a strong argument that DataCorp is a hybrid public authority exercising a public function under s.6(3)(b) HRA 1998 in respect of this activity.

Applying the Aston Cantlow functional test:
(1) Statutory underpinning: the national identity database and data access rights will be governed by statute (e.g. data protection legislation; identity management Acts). DataCorp operates within a statutory framework.
(2) Public funding and accountability: contracted by the Home Office, accountable to the government for performance.
(3) Taking the place of government: the management of a national identity database is a core state function — managing personal data held by the state, determining access to it, and protecting it. This is not a purely commercial function; it is governmental in character.
(4) No purely private contractual relationship with Finn — Finn's relationship with the database is not contractual; it is determined by statute and public law.

Distinguishing YL: in YL the care home operator's relationship with residents was commercial and contractual. DataCorp's management of a state identity database has no equivalent private character — it is managing state data on behalf of the state.

Conclusion: DataCorp is likely a hybrid public authority for this function. Its refusal to provide access must be compatible with Art 8 (right to respect for private life, including the right of access to personal information held about oneself: Gaskin v UK [1989]). The refusal based on 'internal efficiency reasons' is unlikely to be a proportionate interference with Art 8.

⚠️ Contracting out state functions does not extinguish HRA obligations if the function is governmental in character.
S.6 public authorities.summary_line: HYBRID PUBLIC AUTHORITY · FUNCTIONAL TEST · STATE DATABASE · GOVERNMENTAL CHARACTER · YL DISTINGUISHED · ART 8

S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.front: Friends of the Forest, an environmental campaign group, seeks to challenge under the HRA 1998 a planning authority's decision to permit a large development that they argue will harm Art 8 rights (private and family life) of local residents. The residents themselves have not brought any claim. Does Friends of the Forest have standing?
S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.answer:
Friends of the Forest does not have standing to bring an HRA claim under s.7 HRA 1998.

Section 7(1) HRA 1998 provides that a person may bring proceedings under s.6(1) only if they are (or would be) a 'victim' of the unlawful act. The victim test mirrors Art 34 ECHR — it requires the applicant to be directly affected by the alleged violation:
— A direct victim: the person whose rights are directly interfered with.
— A potential victim: a person at real risk of direct interference.
— An indirect victim: typically a close family member of a primary victim.

Friends of the Forest is a campaign group. It is not directly or potentially affected by the planning decision in respect of its own Art 8 rights. A general interest in the outcome — however genuine and well-informed — is not sufficient for the victim test. Campaign groups and pressure groups cannot be victims under s.7.

Contrast with ordinary judicial review: under s.31(3) Senior Courts Act 1981, Friends of the Forest might have sufficient interest (applying R v Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs ex p World Development Movement [1995]), because the 'sufficient interest' test is broader and more purposive. But this would need to be an ordinary JR challenge, not an HRA s.7 claim.

Conclusion: no HRA standing. Friends of the Forest should either support the affected residents in bringing their own s.7 claim, or bring a separate ordinary JR claim on other grounds.

⚠️ The victim test (s.7) is narrower than the sufficient interest test (JR). This is a classic exam distinction.
S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.summary_line: S.7 VICTIM TEST · CAMPAIGN GROUP · NO STANDING · NARROWER THAN JR · SUFFICIENT INTEREST · WDM CONTRAST

S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.front: Helena brings a successful HRA claim against the police under s.6 HRA 1998, establishing that her Art 8 rights were violated by an unlawful surveillance operation. She seeks: (a) a declaration that the surveillance was unlawful; (b) damages of £50,000 for distress; (c) an injunction preventing future surveillance. Advise on her prospects for each remedy.
S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.answer:
(a) Declaration: Helena is very likely to obtain a declaration. Courts routinely make declarations that a specific act was incompatible with a Convention right. A declaration is a flexible, non-coercive remedy that authoritatively states the legal position — it does not require the court to substitute its judgment on complex policy matters. Under s.8(1) HRA 1998, the court may grant any relief within its powers that is just and appropriate.

(b) Damages of £50,000: unlikely to be awarded in full, and potentially not at all. Under s.8(3) HRA 1998, damages may only be awarded if the court is satisfied that the award is necessary to afford just satisfaction — taking into account all the circumstances and the principles applied by the ECtHR under Art 41 ECHR. ECtHR damages are generally modest — typically far below domestic tort awards. Awards of £50,000 for distress alone would be exceptional in Strasbourg jurisprudence. A much lower figure (or a finding that the declaration itself constitutes just satisfaction) is more likely.

(c) Injunction preventing future surveillance: available in principle under s.8(1), but courts are cautious about prospective injunctions against the police in operational matters. Helena would need to demonstrate a real and ongoing risk of future unlawful surveillance — a past breach does not automatically establish a continuing risk. If she can demonstrate this, an injunction may be granted.

⚠️ HRA damages are not automatic and follow the ECtHR's just satisfaction principle — they are typically lower than domestic tort damages.
S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.summary_line: S.8 HRA · DECLARATION LIKELY · DAMAGES LOW · JUST SATISFACTION · ECTHR PRINCIPLES · INJUNCTION CAUTIOUS

S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.front: Tom is serving a mandatory life sentence. He claims the Home Secretary's decision to set his minimum tariff at 25 years, departing upward from the 18 years recommended by the trial judge and without giving reasons, breaches his Art 6 rights. A declaration of incompatibility was made in a similar case. What remedies are available to Tom personally?
S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.answer:
Tom's situation illustrates a key limitation of the HRA 1998's remedial structure.

The declaration of incompatibility: a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 HRA 1998 in a similar case does not give Tom a remedy. Under s.4(6), a declaration: (a) does not affect the validity of the provision; and (b) is not binding on the parties to the proceedings. Tom cannot rely on a declaration made in another case to reduce his tariff — that declaration was between different parties.

Tom's own claim: Tom would need to bring his own proceedings under s.7 HRA 1998 as a victim, arguing that the Home Secretary's specific decision in his case breached Art 6 (right to a fair trial / independent tribunal: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Anderson [2002]).

If Tom's individual claim succeeds:
— The court may make a declaration that the specific decision was incompatible with Art 6.
— Under s.8, the court may grant any relief it considers just and appropriate, potentially including quashing the tariff decision and requiring it to be remade by an independent judge.
— Damages are possible but would follow ECtHR just satisfaction principles — modest.

Practically: following Anderson [2002], the tariff-setting power was transferred to judges by the Criminal Justice Act 2003. Tom should ask whether his tariff was set before or after this reform — if after, a judge set it and there is no Art 6 issue.

⚠️ A declaration in another case is not a remedy for Tom. He must bring his own s.7 claim as a victim.
S.7 standing / s.8 remedies.summary_line: S.4 NOT BINDING ON TOM · OWN S.7 CLAIM · ART 6 · ANDERSON · TARIFF · S.8 REMEDIES · JUST SATISFACTION

Qualified rights / proportionality.front: The local authority bans a pro-fox hunting group from holding a march through the town centre, citing public order concerns. The group argues this breaches Art 11 ECHR (freedom of assembly and association). The authority argues the ban is justified. Advise.
Qualified rights / proportionality.answer:
Art 11 ECHR is a qualified right — it can be interfered with, but only where the interference is: (1) prescribed by law; (2) in pursuit of a legitimate aim; and (3) necessary in a democratic society (proportionate).

Applying the Bank Mellat four-stage proportionality framework:

(1) Prescribed by law: the ban must have a clear and accessible legal basis. Powers to impose conditions on or ban public processions exist under the Public Order Act 1986. If the ban is made under this Act, the prescription requirement is satisfied.

(2) Legitimate aim: Art 11(2) permits restrictions for the prevention of disorder or crime, protection of health or morals, or protection of rights of others. A public order concern can constitute a legitimate aim if it is genuine and not pretextual.

(3) Rational connection: is banning the march rationally connected to preventing public disorder? If there is credible evidence of a serious disorder risk, yes.

(4) Proportionate / least restrictive means: this is the critical stage. A complete ban is the most intrusive response. Could conditions on time, route, or numbers have addressed the public order risk with less interference? Art 11 requires the authority to consider less restrictive alternatives before imposing a total ban.

Conclusion: if the authority imposed a ban without considering less restrictive conditions first, the interference is likely disproportionate and unlawful. The group has a strong argument for judicial review on proportionality grounds under the HRA.

⚠️ A complete ban requires stronger justification than conditions — the least restrictive means principle is central.
Qualified rights / proportionality.summary_line: ART 11 QUALIFIED RIGHT · PROPORTIONALITY · BANK MELLAT · LEAST RESTRICTIVE · PUBLIC ORDER ACT · BAN vs CONDITIONS

Qualified rights / proportionality.front: A journalist, Nina, publishes an article identifying an undercover police officer by name. The police apply for an injunction under Art 8 ECHR (private life / safety of the officer). Nina argues the injunction breaches Art 10 ECHR (freedom of expression). How should the court approach the conflict between the two rights?
Qualified rights / proportionality.answer:
This is a horizontal conflict between two qualified Convention rights — Art 8 (private and family life, including physical safety) and Art 10 (freedom of expression). Neither automatically prevails.

The court must apply the s.12 HRA 1998 framework where prior restraint of publication is sought: the court must have particular regard to the importance of freedom of expression, and must not restrain publication unless it is satisfied the applicant is likely to establish at trial that publication should not be allowed.

Beyond s.12, the court must balance the two rights applying proportionality — neither has automatic priority: Re S (A Child) [2004] (Lord Steyn's four propositions): (1) neither Art 8 nor Art 10 has presumptive priority; (2) where values under both are in play, the court carries out an 'intense focus' on the comparative importance of the rights; (3) justifications for interfering with each are considered; (4) a proportionality balance is struck.

Factors favouring Art 8 (injunction): the officer faces a genuine safety risk — this engages the right to life dimension of Art 8. There is no public interest in identifying the specific individual officer.

Factors favouring Art 10 (no injunction): freedom of the press; public interest in scrutiny of covert policing; the information may already be in the public domain.

Conclusion: on these facts, the officer's safety risk likely outweighs the journalistic interest in naming the specific individual. An injunction is probably justified, but it should be narrowly drawn — restraining identification of the specific officer, not broader reporting on undercover policing.

⚠️ Conflicts between Art 8 and Art 10 require intense focus balancing — neither right wins by default.
Qualified rights / proportionality.summary_line: ART 8 v ART 10 · CONFLICT · RE S · INTENSE FOCUS · S.12 HRA · PROPORTIONALITY BALANCE · NEITHER PREVAILS

Qualified rights / proportionality.front: The government introduces a scheme requiring all foreign nationals to register their address with the police within 48 hours of arriving in the UK. A foreign national, Klara, challenges the scheme as incompatible with Art 8 ECHR. She argues the requirement is disproportionate. Advise.
Qualified rights / proportionality.answer:
The government must justify the registration scheme by satisfying all stages of the Art 8(2) proportionality analysis.

Art 8(1) — is Art 8 engaged? Yes. A requirement to register one's address with police engages the right to respect for private life — it involves the collection and retention of personal data about an individual's whereabouts by the state.

Art 8(2) — can the interference be justified? Applying the Bank Mellat four-stage proportionality framework:

(1) Prescribed by law: the scheme must have a clear legal basis (likely a statutory instrument or primary legislation). Assuming it does, this stage is satisfied.

(2) Legitimate aim: immigration control, prevention of crime, national security — these are all permitted aims under Art 8(2).

(3) Rational connection: a registration requirement is rationally connected to monitoring the location of foreign nationals and managing immigration.

(4) Necessary and proportionate — least restrictive means: this is the key question. Is a 48-hour universal requirement — applying to all foreign nationals without any risk assessment — proportionate? A court would ask: is the requirement calibrated to the actual risk posed by different categories of visitor? Could a less broad requirement (e.g. targeting specific risk categories) achieve the same aim? A blanket requirement with no exceptions may fail here.

Conclusion: the scheme may be disproportionate if it applies universally without any tailored assessment of risk. The government would need to demonstrate why a blanket 48-hour requirement is the minimum necessary to achieve the legitimate aim.

⚠️ Proportionality is not a blanket balancing exercise — the least restrictive means stage requires the state to consider less intrusive alternatives.
Qualified rights / proportionality.summary_line: ART 8 · PRIVATE LIFE · DATA · PROPORTIONALITY · FOUR STAGES · BANK MELLAT · LEAST RESTRICTIVE MEANS

Qualified rights / proportionality.front: A statute prohibits any person convicted of a serious sexual offence from ever working with children, with no mechanism for review or exception. A claimant who was convicted 20 years ago as a teenager seeks to challenge the prohibition as incompatible with Art 8 ECHR. Advise.
Qualified rights / proportionality.answer:
The claimant has a strong case that the absolute prohibition is incompatible with Art 8 ECHR.

Art 8 engagement: the prohibition directly interferes with the claimant's right to private life — it prevents him from pursuing his chosen career and affects how he lives his life. This is clearly within Art 8's scope.

Proportionality analysis:
(1) Prescribed by law: the statutory prohibition satisfies this requirement.
(2) Legitimate aim: protection of children from sexual harm is a compelling and clearly legitimate aim under Art 8(2).
(3) Rational connection: a prohibition on working with children following a conviction for sexual offences is rationally connected to child protection.
(4) Necessary and proportionate (the critical stage): the absolute and lifelong nature of the ban — with no mechanism for review, no consideration of individual circumstances, and no account of rehabilitation or the passage of time — may make it disproportionate. The ECtHR has held that lifelong automatic measures without any possibility of review are generally disproportionate: R (F) v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2010] (notification requirements for sex offenders — the Supreme Court held indefinite registration with no review mechanism violated Art 8).

The claimant's circumstances — convicted as a teenager, 20 years of good conduct — make the proportionality challenge particularly strong. A reviewing mechanism that allows individual assessment would be a less restrictive means of achieving the legitimate aim.

Conclusion: the absolute prohibition without review is likely incompatible with Art 8. A court should: (1) consider whether s.3 can read in a review mechanism; (2) if not, make a s.4 declaration of incompatibility.

⚠️ Absolute bars without individual review are disproportionate — proportionality requires case-by-case assessment in sufficiently different circumstances.
Qualified rights / proportionality.summary_line: ART 8 · ABSOLUTE BAN · NO REVIEW · DISPROPORTIONATE · R(F) · REHABILITATION · S.3/S.4 · INDIVIDUAL ASSESSMENT

Derogations / absolute rights.front: A public emergency is declared following a series of large-scale terrorist attacks. The government detains 50 foreign nationals suspected of terrorism under emergency legislation, without charge or trial, relying on an Art 15 ECHR derogation. Ten of the detainees are British nationals. Advise on the validity of the derogation.
Derogations / absolute rights.answer:
Applying A v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2004] (Belmarsh), the derogation is likely invalid in respect of its application to foreign nationals only, and the inclusion of British nationals is inconsistent with the stated basis for derogation.

Art 15 ECHR requirements: (1) there must be a public emergency threatening the life of the nation — a series of large-scale terrorist attacks is capable of satisfying this, as accepted (just) by the HL majority in Belmarsh; (2) the measures must be strictly required by the exigencies of the situation; (3) the measures must not derogate from absolute rights (Arts 2 (subject to Art 15(2)), 3, 4(1), 7).

Problems with this derogation:
(1) Discrimination: detention applies to both foreign nationals and British nationals, but in Belmarsh the HL found that applying measures only to foreign nationals was discriminatory (Art 14 read with Art 5) because the threat of terrorism was not confined to that group. Here, including British nationals removes the discrimination point — but it does not cure the proportionality problem.
(2) Proportionality / strict necessity: detaining 50 suspects without trial requires strict necessity. Is internment without charge necessary, or could prosecution, bail conditions, or control orders achieve the same aim with less interference? The court will scrutinise this closely.
(3) Non-derogable rights: Art 3 (prohibition on torture/inhuman treatment) is absolute and cannot be derogated from. Any conditions of detention must comply with Art 3 regardless of the emergency.

Conclusion: the public emergency condition may be satisfied, but the derogation will be closely scrutinised for proportionality. It must be strictly required — and the state must demonstrate that prosecution or less restrictive measures were inadequate.

⚠️ Art 3 cannot be derogated from. Detention conditions must comply regardless of the emergency declared.
Derogations / absolute rights.summary_line: ART 15 DEROGATION · BELMARSH · PUBLIC EMERGENCY · STRICT NECESSITY · ART 3 NON-DEROGABLE · PROPORTIONALITY

Derogations / absolute rights.front: An immigration officer is told by a detainee that if he is returned to his home country he will be tortured. The officer believes this is unlikely but carries out the removal anyway. The detainee is subsequently tortured. Advise on liability under the HRA 1998.
Derogations / absolute rights.answer:
The removal is a serious violation of Art 3 ECHR, and the immigration officer and the Home Office (as a core public authority) face liability under s.6 HRA 1998.

Art 3 is an absolute right — it admits of no exceptions or qualifications. The state cannot return a person to a country where there are substantial grounds for believing they will face a real risk of torture or inhuman or degrading treatment: Soering v UK (1989) ECtHR. It does not matter that the officer thought the risk was unlikely — if there were substantial grounds for believing the risk was real, the removal was unlawful.

The legal standard: the question is whether there were substantial grounds for believing a real risk existed, not whether torture was more likely than not. A credible account of risk that was not adequately investigated creates liability.

S.6 HRA 1998: the Home Office (core public authority) acted incompatibly with Art 3 in carrying out the removal. The immigration officer acted on behalf of the Home Office — under the Carltona principle, the Home Office bears responsibility.

Remedies under s.8 HRA 1998: the claimant (if alive and returned) can bring a s.7 claim as a direct victim. Remedies available: damages (applying ECtHR just satisfaction principles — potentially significant given the gravity of the violation); a declaration that the removal was unlawful; and an injunction preventing removal pending any further risk assessment.

Additionally, where Art 3 is engaged by removal, courts will apply anxious scrutiny — this is one of the most intensive standards of review in English law.

⚠️ Art 3 is absolute — no emergency, no public interest, no derogation can justify removal to face torture. The risk threshold is 'real risk', not 'more likely than not.'
Derogations / absolute rights.summary_line: ART 3 ABSOLUTE · REMOVAL · REAL RISK · SOERING · S.6 HOME OFFICE · CARLTONA · S.8 DAMAGES · ANXIOUS SCRUTINY

Illegality.front: What is the ground of illegality in judicial review? Identify four sub-categories.
Illegality.answer:
Illegality is one of the three grounds of judicial review identified by Lord Diplock in Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985] (GCHQ). A decision is illegal where the decision-maker acts beyond or contrary to its legal powers.

Four sub-categories:
(1) Acting outside the scope of the power (ultra vires) — doing something the statute does not authorise: Entick v Carrington (1765).
(2) Using a power for an improper purpose — the power is exercised for a purpose other than that for which it was conferred: Padfield v Minister of Agriculture [1968].
(3) Failing to consider relevant matters or considering irrelevant matters — the decision-maker must take account of the right considerations: Roberts v Hopwood [1925].
(4) Unlawful delegation or abdication — the decision-maker cannot sub-delegate a power that Parliament has entrusted to it, nor can it fetter its discretion by rigid policy application: Lavender v Minister of Housing [1970].

⚠️ Error of law going to jurisdiction is also a species of illegality — getting the law wrong can itself vitiate the decision.
Illegality.summary_line: ILLEGALITY · ULTRA VIRES · IMPROPER PURPOSE · RELEVANT/IRRELEVANT · DELEGATION · GCHQ · PADFIELD

Illegality.front: What is the 'improper purpose' sub-category of illegality? Illustrate with Padfield v Minister of Agriculture [1968].
Illegality.answer:
A public authority must exercise its statutory powers for the purposes for which Parliament conferred them. Using a power for a different, collateral purpose is unlawful even if the decision is otherwise within the technical scope of the power.

Padfield v Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food [1968]:
— Statute gave the Minister discretion to refer complaints to a committee.
— Minister refused to refer a complaint from south-east milk producers, in part to avoid political embarrassment.
— HL held: refusal was unlawful. Parliament had conferred discretion for a purpose (investigating genuine grievances) and the Minister had used it to protect himself from parliamentary criticism — an improper purpose.

Key principle: discretion conferred by statute must be exercised consistently with the statutory purpose, not used to frustrate it.

⚠️ Where a decision-maker has multiple purposes, the improper purpose will vitiate the decision only if it was the dominant or a significant purpose: R v Inner London Education Authority ex p Westminster City Council [1986].

⚠️ Link — Padfield / refusals (see FLK1-CAL-069): the improper purpose principle applies equally to unlawful refusals to exercise a discretion, not only to unlawful acts.
Illegality.summary_line: IMPROPER PURPOSE · PADFIELD · STATUTORY PURPOSE · POLITICAL MOTIVE · DOMINANT PURPOSE TEST · ILEA

Illegality.front: What is the principle against fettering discretion, and when is it breached?
Illegality.answer:
A decision-maker must not fetter its discretion by rigidly applying a policy without considering the individual merits of each case. Each decision must genuinely be made on its own facts.

The principle: a public body is entitled to adopt a policy to guide decision-making — this promotes consistency and efficiency. But it must: (a) make the policy known; and (b) be willing to consider departing from the policy where the individual circumstances warrant it.

Breach:
— Lavender v Minister of Housing [1970]: Minister of Housing automatically deferred to the Minister of Agriculture on all planning applications involving agricultural land, without independent consideration. Held: unlawful fettering.
— British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971]: BoT refused all grants for items costing less than £25, without exception. HL: a policy is fine, but there must always be a willingness to listen to someone with something new to say.

⚠️ Contrast: having a policy ≠ fettering. The question is whether the body has closed its mind to exceptions.
Illegality.summary_line: FETTERING DISCRETION · LAVENDER · BRITISH OXYGEN · POLICY FINE · MUST CONSIDER EXCEPTIONS · CLOSED MIND

Illegality.front: What is the principle of legality, and how does it operate as a limit on statutory interpretation?
Illegality.answer:
The principle of legality: Parliament is presumed not to intend to abrogate fundamental common law rights unless it does so by express words or necessary implication.

Key cases:
— R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Pierson [1998]: HL held Parliament does not legislate in a vacuum — statutes are presumed to be enacted against the backdrop of common law rights.
— R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Simms [1999]: Lord Hoffmann — fundamental rights cannot be overridden by general or ambiguous words. The more fundamental the right, the clearer the statutory language must be.

Practical effect: courts interpret ambiguous statutory powers narrowly to avoid infringement of common law rights (access to courts, legal professional privilege, personal liberty).

⚠️ The principle of legality operates at the level of statutory interpretation — it does not allow courts to strike down clear statutory words. If Parliament expressly removes a right, courts must apply that. Contrast with s.3 HRA 1998.

⚠️ Link — Principle of legality and rule of law (see FLK1-CAL-053): the principle of legality operates as both a JR illegality ground (CAL-032) and a constitutional principle rooted in the rule of law (CAL-053). Both dimensions are relevant in exam questions.
Illegality.summary_line: PRINCIPLE OF LEGALITY · PIERSON · SIMMS · FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS · EXPRESS WORDS · NARROW INTERPRETATION

Illegality.front: What is the distinction between jurisdictional errors and non-jurisdictional errors of law, and why does it matter after Anisminic?
Illegality.answer:
Pre-Anisminic: a distinction was drawn between:
— Jurisdictional errors (going to the vires of the decision) — reviewable
— Non-jurisdictional errors of law within jurisdiction — not reviewable

Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969]: the HL effectively collapsed this distinction. A decision that is wrong in law is a nullity — it is not a true determination at all. Therefore almost any error of law can be characterised as jurisdictional and is reviewable.

Post-Anisminic position: confirmed in R v Hull University Visitor ex p Page [1993] — there is a single category of reviewable error of law (subject to limited exceptions for bodies with truly exclusive jurisdiction to determine their own law, e.g. university visitors — since abolished).

Practical effect:
— Courts now review errors of law by all public decision-makers.
— The category of non-reviewable error of law is effectively extinct in the ordinary judicial review context.

⚠️ However, the court gives varying degrees of deference to specialist tribunals and expert bodies on questions within their particular expertise — the error must still be material.
Illegality.summary_line: JURISDICTIONAL ERROR · ANISMINIC · ERROR OF LAW = NULLITY · PAGE · SINGLE CATEGORY · DEFERENCE

Illegality.front: What is the rule against unlawful sub-delegation in administrative law?
Illegality.answer:
The Latin maxim delegatus non potest delegare ('a delegate cannot delegate') provides that where Parliament has entrusted a specific power to a particular decision-maker, that decision-maker must exercise it personally and cannot further delegate it to another body.

Rationale: Parliament chose the specific body for a reason — delegating to an unauthorised body circumvents Parliament's intention.

Key cases:
— Lavender v Minister of Housing [1970]: unlawful sub-delegation where the Minister automatically deferred all decisions to another minister, rendering the statutory discretion meaningless.
— Barnard v National Dock Labour Board [1953]: the Board unlawfully delegated disciplinary power to a port manager — the power was conferred on the Board, not on the manager.

Distinct from the Carltona principle (see FLK1-CAL-051): under Carltona, ministers can delegate to civil servants within their department because those officials act as the minister's alter ego — this is not sub-delegation but exercise of the power through departmental machinery.

⚠️ The question is always: has the decision-maker genuinely exercised the statutory discretion, or has it handed the decision to an unauthorised person?

⚠️ EXAM TRAP: Distinguish unlawful sub-delegation from the Carltona principle. Under Carltona, a civil servant exercises the minister's power as the minister's alter ego — this is not sub-delegation; it is constitutionally authorised exercise of the power through departmental machinery. The minister remains legally responsible. ⚠️ Link — Carltona principle (see FLK1-CAL-051): ministerial delegation within the department is lawful; delegation outside it is not.
Illegality.summary_line: SUB-DELEGATION · DELEGATUS NON POTEST · LAVENDER · BARNARD · UNLAWFUL · CARLTONA DISTINCTION

Illegality.front: What is the significance of Padfield v Minister of Agriculture [1968] for the exercise of statutory discretion?
Illegality.answer:
Padfield v Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food [1968] is the leading case on the requirement that statutory discretionary powers must be exercised consistently with the purpose for which Parliament conferred them.

Facts: The Agriculture Act 1958 empowered the Minister to refer complaints to an independent committee. South-east milk producers complained about the pricing formula. The Minister refused to refer the complaint, partly to avoid political embarrassment if the committee ruled against him.

Held (HL): The Minister had acted unlawfully. Though the statute gave him a wide discretion ('if the Minister in any case so directs'), that discretion had to be exercised in conformity with the statutory purpose — which was to enable genuine grievances to be investigated. Using the discretion to protect the Minister from political criticism frustrated the statutory purpose.

Key principles:
(1) Wide discretionary language does not mean the discretion is unlimited.
(2) Statutory discretion must be used for the statutory purpose — not to frustrate it.
(3) Refusing to act for an improper purpose is just as unlawful as acting for an improper purpose.

⚠️ Padfield applies both to acts and refusals — a minister cannot refuse to exercise a power simply because exercising it would be inconvenient.

⚠️ Link — Improper purpose (see FLK1-CAL-030): Padfield is the leading case on both the general improper purpose principle and on the specific application to refusals. Read the two cards together.
Illegality.summary_line: PADFIELD 1968 · STATUTORY PURPOSE · IMPROPER PURPOSE · WIDE DISCRETION NOT UNLIMITED · REFUSAL ALSO UNLAWFUL

Illegality.front: Padfield v Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food [1968]
Illegality.issue: Whether the Minister of Agriculture acted unlawfully by refusing to refer a complaint from south-east milk producers to the Milk Marketing Board's committee of investigation.
Illegality.rule: A statutory discretion must be exercised for the purposes for which it was conferred by Parliament. Using a discretion — including a discretion to refuse — for an improper purpose (here, avoiding political embarrassment) is unlawful. Wide or apparently unqualified discretionary language does not mean the discretion is unlimited; it must still be exercised consistently with the statutory objective.
Illegality.application: The statute empowered the Minister to refer complaints 'if the Minister in any case so directs.' The Minister refused, partly to avoid political difficulties if the committee upheld the complaint. The HL held this was unlawful — the discretion existed to enable genuine grievances to be investigated; using it to protect ministerial interests frustrated the statutory purpose.
Illegality.conclusion: The refusal was unlawful; mandamus issued. Padfield is the foundational authority for the improper purpose ground of illegality. It establishes that: (1) wide discretion is not unfettered discretion; (2) refusals as well as acts can be challenged; (3) the dominant purpose of the exercise must be consistent with the statutory aim.
Illegality.summary_line: PADFIELD · IMPROPER PURPOSE · STATUTORY DISCRETION · REFUSAL UNLAWFUL · POLITICAL MOTIVE · MANDAMUS

Illegality.front: Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969]
Illegality.issue: Whether an error of law by the Foreign Compensation Commission in determining Anisminic's claim was protected by an ouster clause, and whether a legally erroneous determination was a 'determination' at all.
Illegality.rule: A decision that is wrong in law — because the decision-maker misconstrued its jurisdiction or made a jurisdictional error — is a nullity. A nullity is not a 'determination' at all. Therefore an ouster clause protecting 'determinations' of the Commission from challenge does not protect a nullity, because a nullity was never a determination in the first place. Courts will interpret ouster clauses narrowly.
Illegality.application: The FCC misconstrued the relevant Order in Council, applying a condition (that a successor in title must be a British national) that did not apply to Anisminic's case. The FCC's error went to jurisdiction. The ouster clause in the Foreign Compensation Act 1950 purported to exclude review. The HL held the clause could not protect a nullity — the FCC had not made a 'determination' but had asked itself the wrong question.
Illegality.conclusion: The FCC's decision was a nullity and was quashed. Anisminic effectively collapsed the distinction between jurisdictional and non-jurisdictional errors of law — almost any error of law can now be characterised as taking the decision-maker outside its jurisdiction. Ouster clauses are read as not protecting nullities.
Illegality.summary_line: ANISMINIC · ERROR OF LAW · NULLITY · OUSTER CLAUSE · JURISDICTION · DETERMINATION · JUDICIAL REVIEW CANNOT BE EXCLUDED

Illegality.front: Carltona Ltd v Commissioners of Works [1943]
Illegality.issue: Whether a civil servant in the Ministry of Works had authority to requisition premises on behalf of the Commissioners of Works, where the relevant Defence Regulation gave the power to the Commissioners themselves.
Illegality.rule: Where a statute confers a power on a minister or government department, that power may be exercised by an official within the department on the minister's behalf. The official acts as the minister's alter ego — the minister remains legally responsible for the decision. This is not unlawful sub-delegation but a constitutional necessity given the volume of ministerial decisions.
Illegality.application: A civil servant signed a requisition order. Carltona argued that only the Commissioners themselves could exercise the power. The Court of Appeal rejected this — government could not function if ministers had to personally make every decision. Officials act for and in the name of the minister; the minister is legally accountable.
Illegality.conclusion: The requisition was lawful. The Carltona principle permits intra-departmental delegation as a matter of constitutional practice. The limits are: (1) it does not extend outside the department; (2) statute may require the minister to act personally; (3) the minister remains constitutionally responsible.
Illegality.summary_line: CARLTONA · MINISTERIAL DELEGATION · CIVIL SERVANT · ALTER EGO · CONSTITUTIONAL NECESSITY · MINISTER RESPONSIBLE

Illegality.front: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Simms [1999]
Illegality.issue: Whether prison rules preventing prisoners from giving oral interviews to journalists investigating alleged miscarriages of justice were compatible with the right to freedom of expression.
Illegality.rule: The principle of legality: Parliament is presumed not to legislate in a way that would remove fundamental rights unless it does so by express words or necessary implication. Fundamental rights cannot be overridden by general or ambiguous statutory language. The more fundamental the right, the more clearly Parliament must express an intention to abrogate it. General words in a statute, even if technically wide enough to cover a restriction of fundamental rights, will not be read as authorising that restriction.
Illegality.application: Prison rules made under a general statutory power restricted prisoners from giving interviews to journalists. The HL held the general statutory power did not authorise this specific restriction — it impinged on the fundamental right of freedom of expression (necessary to correct miscarriages of justice) without sufficiently clear parliamentary authority.
Illegality.conclusion: The restrictions were unlawful. Lord Hoffmann's speech in Simms is the definitive statement of the principle of legality in English law: 'fundamental rights cannot be overridden by general or ambiguous words.' It is a tool of statutory interpretation — not a power to strike down legislation — but one that operates with considerable force.
Illegality.summary_line: SIMMS · PRINCIPLE OF LEGALITY · LORD HOFFMANN · FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS · GENERAL WORDS INSUFFICIENT · EXPRESS ABROGATION

Illegality.front: A local authority has a statutory power under the Housing Act to 'allocate housing to persons in housing need.' It passes a resolution that it will only allocate housing to persons who have lived in the borough for at least five years. A new arrival to the borough, Fatima, applies for housing and is refused solely because she has not lived there for five years. Advise.
Illegality.answer:
The local authority has acted unlawfully on two grounds of illegality.

(1) Ultra vires — acting outside the scope of the power: the statutory power is to allocate housing to persons 'in housing need.' The Act does not authorise an additional residence requirement. Imposing a five-year residence condition as an absolute bar adds a qualification to the power that Parliament did not enact. Fatima's refusal on this basis is ultra vires the statute.

(2) Fettering discretion: even if a local authority may adopt a policy to guide allocation decisions (British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971]), the policy must be applied flexibly — the authority must always be willing to consider individual circumstances and depart from the policy where the case warrants it. A blanket resolution that operates as an absolute rule, with no exception for individual circumstances, constitutes an unlawful fettering of the authority's discretion: Lavender v Minister of Housing [1970].

Remedy: Fatima should apply for judicial review seeking a quashing order to quash the refusal of her application, and a mandatory order requiring the authority to consider her application lawfully — assessing her actual housing need under the statutory criteria without applying the unlawful residence requirement.

⚠️ A policy is permissible; an absolute rule with no exceptions is not. The difference is whether the decision-maker genuinely considers individual circumstances.
Illegality.summary_line: ULTRA VIRES · FETTERING DISCRETION · RESIDENCE REQUIREMENT · BRITISH OXYGEN · LAVENDER · QUASHING · MANDATORY ORDER

Illegality.front: The Minister of Culture has a statutory power to award grants to 'arts organisations that promote British cultural heritage.' She refuses a grant application from the Modern Dance Company, citing in her written decision that 'modern dance does not represent traditional British culture' and also that 'the Company's director made critical public comments about government arts policy.' Advise.
Illegality.answer:
The Minister has acted unlawfully on the improper purpose ground of illegality.

The statutory power is to fund organisations that promote British cultural heritage. The first reason given — that modern dance does not represent traditional culture — may be a lawful exercise of the discretion (a judgment about what constitutes British cultural heritage) provided it is not irrational.

However, the second reason — that the director made critical public comments about government policy — is plainly an improper purpose. Penalising an organisation because its leadership criticised the government has nothing to do with the promotion of British cultural heritage. It is using the statutory discretion for a collateral, punitive purpose that Parliament did not authorise: Padfield v Minister of Agriculture [1968].

Where a decision is made for both proper and improper purposes, the court asks whether the improper purpose was the dominant or a significant (not merely incidental) purpose: R v ILEA ex p Westminster City Council [1986]. On these facts, the critical comments appear to have featured prominently in the written decision — it is likely to be treated as a significant purpose, which would vitiate the decision.

The Minister should also be advised that penalising an organisation for critical speech may engage Art 10 ECHR (freedom of expression) — a proportionality analysis would be required.

Remedy: the Modern Dance Company should seek a quashing order and a mandatory order requiring the Minister to reconsider the application without the improper purpose.

⚠️ Multiple purposes: if an improper purpose is dominant or significant, the decision is vitiated — not merely if it was the sole purpose.
Illegality.summary_line: IMPROPER PURPOSE · PADFIELD · DOMINANT PURPOSE · ILEA · ART 10 · QUASHING · SIGNIFICANT PURPOSE

Illegality.front: The Licensing Authority has a statutory power to grant or refuse licences to nightclubs. It has adopted a policy of refusing all applications for new nightclubs in the town centre. When Beatrix applies for a licence, the officer simply stamps her application 'REFUSED — Town Centre Policy' without reading her application. Advise.
Illegality.answer:
The Licensing Authority has unlawfully fettered its discretion and failed to consider the individual merits of Beatrix's application.

The principle from British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971] (Lord Reid) confirms that a public body may adopt a policy to guide its decisions — this promotes consistency. However, it must: (a) be willing to make the policy known; and (b) always be prepared to consider whether individual circumstances justify departing from the policy.

Here, the officer did not read Beatrix's application at all — there was no genuine individual consideration. The policy was applied as an absolute, irrebuttable rule. This is the paradigm case of unlawful fettering: Lavender v Minister of Housing [1970]. The decision-maker has closed its mind entirely to exceptions, which is not a lawful exercise of statutory discretion.

The failure to read the application also independently constitutes a breach of the duty to act fairly (procedural impropriety) — an applicant is entitled to have their application genuinely considered on its merits.

Remedy: Beatrix should apply for judicial review. A quashing order will set aside the refusal. A mandatory order will require the Authority to consider her application properly — assessing its individual merits — though it is entitled to refuse again if the reasons are lawful.

⚠️ The Authority is not required to grant the licence — it is required to genuinely consider whether to grant it. Closed-mind refusal is the vice, not refusal itself.
Illegality.summary_line: FETTERING · BRITISH OXYGEN · LAVENDER · CLOSED MIND · NO INDIVIDUAL CONSIDERATION · QUASHING · MANDATORY ORDER

Illegality.front: The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) has a statutory duty to inspect premises where dangerous chemicals are stored. It delegates the inspection function to approved private contractors. One of those contractors inspects a factory and issues a certificate of compliance. The factory later explodes, injuring workers. Was the delegation lawful?
Illegality.answer:
The delegation is potentially unlawful, depending on whether the HSE's enabling statute authorised it.

The principle of delegatus non potest delegare: a body to which Parliament has entrusted a specific statutory function cannot further sub-delegate that function to another body unless Parliament has expressly or impliedly authorised such delegation: Barnard v National Dock Labour Board [1953].

The key question is whether the HSE's statute authorises delegation of the inspection function to private contractors. If the Act expressly provides for approved inspectors, the delegation is authorised. If the statute confers the duty on the HSE specifically and does not provide for delegation, handing the function to private contractors would be an unlawful sub-delegation — the private contractors have no statutory authority.

Distinguish Carltona: the Carltona principle allows HSE officers (as civil servants in a government agency) to act on behalf of the HSE — this is intra-agency exercise of the power, not sub-delegation. But delegation to independent private companies is a different matter — they are outside the agency structure.

Consequence if unlawful: the certificates of compliance issued by the unauthorised contractors would be nullities. The HSE would remain under its statutory duty to inspect and would be liable for failing to discharge it. The injured workers may have a claim against the HSE for breach of statutory duty or negligence.

⚠️ The delegation issue is separate from the negligence issue — both may arise, but the public law question is whether delegation was lawful in the first place.
Illegality.summary_line: SUB-DELEGATION · DELEGATUS · BARNARD · ENABLING STATUTE · CARLTONA DISTINGUISHED · NULLITY · HSE

Illegality.front: An immigration tribunal has power under the Immigration Act to grant or refuse leave to remain. The statute says the tribunal 'shall have regard to all relevant circumstances.' The tribunal refuses an application, stating in its decision: 'We have considered all relevant factors. However, we were not able to take account of the applicant's medical evidence because our procedural rules prohibit consideration of late evidence, and the medical evidence was submitted after the deadline.' Advise.
Illegality.answer:
The tribunal has acted unlawfully on grounds of illegality — specifically, by taking into account an irrelevant consideration (its procedural rule) to preclude consideration of a relevant one (medical evidence the statute requires it to consider).

The statutory duty is to 'have regard to all relevant circumstances.' Medical evidence about the applicant's health is plainly a relevant circumstance in an immigration decision — health factors can affect the proportionality of removal, engage Art 3 ECHR (inhuman treatment), and are within the scope of 'relevant circumstances' that Parliament intended the tribunal to consider.

The tribunal cannot lawfully use a procedural rule to exclude evidence that the statute requires it to consider. Parliament has defined the scope of relevant considerations — the tribunal cannot narrow that scope by its own procedural rules: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Doody [1993] principle (decision-makers must apply the correct legal framework). Considering irrelevant matters (the late evidence rule) to exclude relevant ones (medical evidence) is a classic illegality error.

Further, if the late evidence was served late because of circumstances beyond the applicant's control (e.g. a medical emergency), rigid application of the rule may also constitute a breach of the duty to act fairly under Art 6 ECHR.

Remedy: the decision should be quashed and remitted for reconsideration with the medical evidence included.

⚠️ Procedural rules cannot be used to exclude evidence that statute requires to be considered — this is relevant/irrelevant considerations illegality.
Illegality.summary_line: IRRELEVANT CONSIDERATIONS · RELEVANT MATTERS · STATUTORY DUTY · PROCEDURAL RULE CANNOT EXCLUDE · DOODY · QUASH AND REMIT

Illegality.front: The Gaming Commission has power under the Gambling Act to revoke a casino's licence if 'the licensee is no longer a fit and proper person.' It revokes the licence of Neptune Casino Ltd after the managing director is convicted of a minor tax offence. No hearing was held before the decision. Neptune argues the decision was unlawful on grounds of illegality and procedural impropriety. Advise.
Illegality.answer:
Neptune has strong grounds on both illegality and procedural impropriety.

Illegality — misinterpretation of 'fit and proper person': the Commission must have applied the correct legal test. A minor tax offence by the managing director is relevant but must be weighed in the context of the overall fitness of the company. If the Commission treated any criminal conviction as automatically making the licensee unfit (regardless of seriousness), it may have: (a) applied the wrong legal test; or (b) failed to take into account relevant matters (the minor nature of the offence, the absence of prior record, the company's compliance history). This would be an error of law going to jurisdiction: Anisminic [1969].

Illegality — fettering: if the Commission applied a blanket rule that any conviction = revocation without individual assessment, this is unlawful fettering of its discretion: British Oxygen.

Procedural impropriety — failure to give a hearing: the revocation of a casino licence is a decision that significantly affects Neptune's commercial interests and livelihood. Ridge v Baldwin [1964] establishes that where statutory powers are exercised to remove an existing right or licence, the affected party is entitled to notice of the case against it and an opportunity to respond before the decision is made. No hearing was held — this is a clear breach of the duty to act fairly.

Remedy: Neptune should seek a quashing order. The Commission must reconsider the revocation following a fair hearing and applying the correct legal test.

⚠️ Licence revocation = significant existing right. Procedural fairness requires notice + opportunity to respond — not merely post-decision reasons.
Illegality.summary_line: FIT AND PROPER · ILLEGALITY · FETTERING · RIDGE v BALDWIN · HEARING REQUIRED · PROCEDURAL IMPROPRIETY · QUASH

Illegality.front: Going deeper: Is the principle of legality (R v Secretary of State ex p Simms [1999]) developing into a substantive constitutional constraint on parliamentary power — or is it merely a sophisticated tool of statutory interpretation?
Illegality.answer:
The principle of legality — that Parliament is presumed not to abrogate fundamental rights without express language — has undergone significant development since Lord Hoffmann's celebrated formulation in Simms [1999]. Whether it has crossed the line from interpretive principle to constitutional constraint is the central question.

The interpretive account: on the traditional view, the principle of legality is simply a strong canon of statutory construction. Parliament can and does remove fundamental rights — it just needs to use clear words. The principle adds nothing to the substantive reach of rights; it merely raises the drafting threshold. This is consistent with parliamentary sovereignty, since Parliament retains the ultimate power: Simms; Pierson [1998].

The constitutional constraint account: a more radical reading — developed by Lord Reed in Privacy International [2019] and anticipated by Laws LJ in ex p Witham [1998] and R (UNISON) v Lord Chancellor [2017] — treats access to courts as a fundamental constitutional right so deeply embedded that even clear statutory language may not suffice to remove it. In UNISON, the Supreme Court struck down Employment Tribunal fee regulations because they undermined the constitutional right of access to justice — without formally relying on any external rights document. This approach treats the principle of legality as a source of substantive constitutional protection, not merely an interpretive presumption.

The scholarly debate: TRS Allan (Law, Liberty and Justice, 1993) argues that the rule of law imposes substantive limits on legislative power — Parliament cannot legislate inconsistently with the rule of law, and courts should say so. Jeffrey Jowell ('Of Vires and Vacuums' [1999] PL) agrees that fundamental rights constrain statutory interpretation in a way that approaches substantive review. Paul Craig and Mark Elliott maintain that UNISON represents judicial creativity straining at the margins of legality — the SC used interpretive tools creatively rather than asserting a substantive power to override statute.

The unresolved trajectory: the sequence Simms → UNISON → Privacy International shows courts progressively expanding the principle. Lord Reed's suggestion in Privacy International that courts might refuse to apply legislation that destroys the ability to challenge administrative decisions in court moves perilously close to the common law constitutionalist position — and closer than any court has previously been to asserting a power to invalidate primary legislation on constitutional grounds.
Illegality.summary_line: PRINCIPLE OF LEGALITY · SIMMS · UNISON · PRIVACY INTERNATIONAL · LORD REED · ALLAN · JOWELL · CONSTITUTIONAL CONSTRAINT

Irrationality.front: What is the Wednesbury unreasonableness test, and from which case does it derive?
Irrationality.answer:
Wednesbury unreasonableness derives from Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation [1948].

Lord Greene MR: a decision will be unlawful if it is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority properly directing itself on the relevant law and facts could have reached it.

The formulation was refined in GCHQ [1985] by Lord Diplock as 'irrationality': a decision that 'defies comprehension' or is so outrageous that no sensible person who has applied his mind to the question could have arrived at it.

The test is high — courts are not substituting their own judgment for that of the decision-maker. They are asking whether the decision falls within the range of reasonable responses.

Examples:
— Short v Poole Corporation [1926]: dismissing a red-haired teacher for having red hair — the paradigm example.
— R v North and East Devon Health Authority ex p Coughlan [2001]: decision to close a care facility given an earlier promise was Wednesbury unreasonable.

⚠️ Wednesbury is a high threshold. The test is more intensive where fundamental rights are engaged — 'heightened scrutiny.'
Irrationality.summary_line: WEDNESBURY 1948 · IRRATIONALITY · GCHQ · NO REASONABLE AUTHORITY · HIGH THRESHOLD · HEIGHTENED SCRUTINY

Irrationality.front: How does proportionality differ from Wednesbury unreasonableness in judicial review?
Irrationality.answer:
Wednesbury unreasonableness: courts ask whether the decision falls outside the range of reasonable responses — highly deferential, court does not substitute its own view.

Proportionality: a structured four-stage inquiry (Bank Mellat v HM Treasury (No 2) [2013]):
(1) Is the objective sufficiently important to justify limiting the right?
(2) Is the measure rationally connected to that objective?
(3) Could a less restrictive measure achieve the same objective?
(4) Is a fair balance struck between the rights of the individual and the interests of the community?

Proportionality is a more intensive standard — it requires the court to scrutinise whether the measure was the minimum necessary to achieve the aim.

When does each apply?
— Convention rights engaged: proportionality applies (HRA 1998 context).
— No Convention right: Wednesbury applies (although courts have moved toward proportionality in many domestic contexts).
— Pham v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2015] — SC acknowledged the shift but did not fully abandon Wednesbury for non-Convention cases.

⚠️ The distinction matters for outcomes — proportionality leads to more intensive review and more frequent findings of unlawfulness.

⚠️ THRESHOLD PRECISION: The Wednesbury threshold is that the decision must be 'so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it' (Lord Greene MR). This is a high bar — courts are not substituting their own judgment.
Irrationality.summary_line: PROPORTIONALITY v WEDNESBURY · BANK MELLAT · FOUR STAGES · MINIMUM NECESSARY · CONVENTION RIGHTS

Irrationality.front: What is 'heightened scrutiny' in Wednesbury review, and when does it apply?
Irrationality.answer:
Heightened (or 'super') Wednesbury scrutiny applies where the decision infringes fundamental rights even in a non-Convention context. In these cases the irrationality threshold is lower — a less extreme degree of unreasonableness suffices to make the decision unlawful.

Development:
— R v Ministry of Defence ex p Smith [1996]: ban on gay service personnel. CA applied heightened Wednesbury — 'the more substantial the interference with human rights, the more the court will require by way of justification before it is satisfied that the decision is reasonable.' But still upheld the decision.
— R v Lord Saville ex p A [2000]: disclosure of soldiers' identities at the Bloody Sunday inquiry engaged Art 2 (right to life) — court applied very intensive scrutiny.

Effect: as fundamental rights move toward the Convention rights framework under the HRA, heightened Wednesbury is increasingly subsumed into proportionality analysis.

⚠️ The Smith case illustrates the limit of heightened Wednesbury — it was subsequently found to breach Art 8 at Strasbourg (Lustig-Prean v UK [1999]) because Wednesbury, even heightened, was insufficient protection.

⚠️ THRESHOLD PRECISION: The Wednesbury threshold is that the decision must be 'so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it' (Lord Greene MR). This is a high bar — courts are not substituting their own judgment.
Irrationality.summary_line: HEIGHTENED SCRUTINY · SMITH · FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS · LOWER THRESHOLD · SUBSUMED BY PROPORTIONALITY

Irrationality.front: What was decided in R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Brind [1991], and what does it show about the relationship between Wednesbury and Convention rights pre-HRA?
Irrationality.answer:
R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Brind [1991]:

Facts: The Home Secretary issued directives under the Broadcasting Act 1981 banning broadcasters from transmitting the voices of terrorist organisation spokespersons (IRA members). Broadcasters challenged this as an infringement of Art 10 ECHR (freedom of expression).

Held (HL): The directives were lawful. Under Wednesbury, the courts would not second-guess the Home Secretary's judgment. The ECHR was not incorporated into domestic law (pre-HRA) and could not be directly relied upon. Convention rights could inform statutory interpretation where ambiguous, but could not override a discretionary decision that was not Wednesbury unreasonable.

Significance:
(1) Demonstrated the pre-HRA gap — Convention rights were not enforceable in domestic courts.
(2) Showed the limits of Wednesbury in protecting rights — a wide ministerial discretion would stand unless grossly unreasonable.
(3) Spurred the case for the HRA 1998.

⚠️ Post-HRA, such a directive would now be tested under proportionality (Art 10 qualified right), and would be much more difficult to justify.

⚠️ THRESHOLD PRECISION: The Wednesbury threshold is that the decision must be 'so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it' (Lord Greene MR). This is a high bar — courts are not substituting their own judgment.
Irrationality.summary_line: BRIND 1991 · PRE-HRA · WEDNESBURY · ECHR NOT INCORPORATED · CONVENTION INFORMS INTERPRETATION ONLY

Irrationality.front: What is the current status of proportionality as a ground of judicial review in domestic (non-HRA) cases?
Irrationality.answer:
The relationship between proportionality and Wednesbury unreasonableness in domestic (non-Convention) judicial review is one of the most actively contested areas of administrative law.

Traditional position: proportionality applies only where a Convention right is engaged (HRA context). Ordinary domestic JR uses Wednesbury: R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Brind [1991].

Evolution:
— R (Daly) v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2001]: Lord Steyn drew a distinction between heightened Wednesbury and proportionality — they are different in substance. Proportionality requires the court to assess whether the measure was strictly necessary.
— Pham v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2015]: SC acknowledged proportionality may be becoming a general ground of review in domestic law, but declined to resolve the question definitively.
— R (Youssef) v Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs [2016]: SC noted the convergence of Wednesbury and proportionality but left the question open.
— R (SC, CB) v Secretary of State for Work and Pensions [2021]: SC applied proportionality in a domestic (non-HRA) context — significant step toward general adoption.

Current position: Wednesbury remains the formal domestic standard, but proportionality is increasingly applied in practice, particularly where fundamental rights are at stake. The distinction is narrowing but not yet abolished.

⚠️ In an SQE SBA: if a Convention right is engaged, use proportionality. If not, apply Wednesbury but note heightened scrutiny if fundamental interests are at stake.

⚠️ THRESHOLD PRECISION: The Wednesbury threshold is that the decision must be 'so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it' (Lord Greene MR). This is a high bar — courts are not substituting their own judgment.
Irrationality.summary_line: PROPORTIONALITY DOMESTIC · WEDNESBURY DEFAULT · DALY · PHAM · SC v SSWP · CONVERGENCE · NOT RESOLVED

Irrationality.front: Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation [1948]
Irrationality.issue: Whether the Wednesbury Corporation's condition — that no children under 15 should be admitted on Sundays — attached to a licence for Sunday film screenings was unlawful as unreasonable.
Irrationality.rule: A decision of a public authority will be unlawful on grounds of unreasonableness if it is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority, properly directing itself on the relevant law and facts, could have reached it. The court is not substituting its own view for that of the decision-maker; it asks only whether the decision falls outside the range of reasonable responses available.
Irrationality.application: The cinema company argued the condition was unreasonable. The Court of Appeal held that the condition was within the range of reasonable responses available to the licensing authority. The court would not interfere simply because it might have decided differently — the threshold for intervention is high.
Irrationality.conclusion: The condition was upheld. Wednesbury established the foundational irrationality test in English administrative law. Lord Greene MR's formulation ('so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could have reached it') remains the classic statement, though it has been qualified by: (1) heightened Wednesbury for fundamental rights cases; (2) proportionality where Convention rights are engaged.
Irrationality.summary_line: WEDNESBURY · UNREASONABLENESS · LORD GREENE · RANGE OF RESPONSES · HIGH THRESHOLD · NOT SUBSTITUTION

Irrationality.front: R v Ministry of Defence ex p Smith [1996]
Irrationality.issue: Whether the Ministry of Defence's policy banning homosexuals from serving in the armed forces was Wednesbury unreasonable, and what standard of review applied where fundamental rights were engaged.
Irrationality.rule: Where a decision interferes with fundamental rights, the court applies a more intensive form of Wednesbury review — 'heightened scrutiny.' The more substantial the interference with human rights, the more the court will require by way of justification. However, in areas involving national security or military policy, the court retains considerable deference to the executive's assessment.
Irrationality.application: The CA acknowledged that the policy engaged fundamental rights of the applicants (their right to serve and their sexuality). Applying heightened Wednesbury, the court still upheld the ban — the MoD had put forward a rational basis (potential disruption to unit cohesion and morale) which the court was not in a position to gainsay in the military context.
Irrationality.conclusion: The policy was upheld domestically. However, the ECtHR later found a violation of Art 8 in Lustig-Prean and Beckett v UK [1999] — the Strasbourg standard was more demanding than even heightened Wednesbury. This demonstrated the gap between Wednesbury (even heightened) and proportionality, and contributed to the case for the HRA.
Irrationality.summary_line: EX P SMITH · HEIGHTENED WEDNESBURY · FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS · MILITARY DEFERENCE · ART 8 STRASBOURG · GAP

Irrationality.front: R (Daly) v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2001]
Irrationality.issue: Whether a Prison Service policy requiring prisoners to be absent when their legally privileged correspondence was examined by prison officers was compatible with their right to legal professional privilege and Art 8 ECHR.
Irrationality.rule: Proportionality requires the court to assess whether the measure was strictly necessary to achieve the legitimate aim — it is a more intensive standard than Wednesbury. In a case involving Convention rights, courts apply proportionality, not Wednesbury. There is a material difference between the two: proportionality may require closer examination of the balance struck even where the decision is not irrational.
Irrationality.application: The HL held the policy was unlawful. Lord Steyn drew a sharp distinction between heightened Wednesbury and proportionality: Wednesbury asks whether the decision is within a range of responses; proportionality asks whether the specific measure adopted was strictly necessary. The policy went further than necessary — prisoners need not be present for inspection of legally privileged correspondence.
Irrationality.conclusion: The policy was quashed. Daly is the clearest judicial statement of the distinction between Wednesbury and proportionality and confirms that proportionality applies where Convention rights are engaged. Lord Steyn's analysis has been widely followed.
Irrationality.summary_line: DALY · PROPORTIONALITY v WEDNESBURY · LORD STEYN · STRICTLY NECESSARY · LEGAL PROFESSIONAL PRIVILEGE · ART 8

Irrationality.issue: Whether a Treasury direction under the Counter-Terrorism Act 2008 prohibiting all UK financial institutions from dealing with Bank Mellat was a proportionate means of achieving the statutory aim of preventing nuclear weapons proliferation.
Irrationality.rule: The proportionality test in judicial review (applicable where a fundamental right or Convention right is engaged) involves four stages: (1) Is the objective sufficiently important to justify limiting the right? (2) Is the measure rationally connected to that objective? (3) Could a less restrictive measure have been used without unacceptably compromising the objective? (4) Has a fair balance been struck between the rights of the individual and the interests of the community?
Irrationality.application: The SC applied the four-stage proportionality framework. The Treasury's direction targeted Bank Mellat alone, though there was no evidence it posed a greater risk than other Iranian banks. The measure failed at stages (3) and (4) — it was not the least restrictive available means and the balance struck between the individual burden and the public benefit was not fair.
Irrationality.conclusion: The direction was disproportionate and quashed. Bank Mellat is the leading SC authority for the four-stage proportionality framework in domestic judicial review. It is cited in all subsequent proportionality analyses and represents the clearest statement of the test in English law.
Irrationality.summary_line: BANK MELLAT · FOUR-STAGE PROPORTIONALITY · RATIONAL CONNECTION · LEAST RESTRICTIVE · FAIR BALANCE · QUASHED

Irrationality.front: A local authority planning committee refuses planning permission for a new supermarket in a residential area. Its stated reason is that 'supermarkets are inherently bad for communities and we disapprove of large retail chains.' The developer seeks judicial review. Advise on the ground of irrationality.
Irrationality.answer:
The planning committee's decision is likely unlawful on grounds of irrationality (Wednesbury unreasonableness) and also independently on grounds of illegality (irrelevant considerations and improper purpose).

Wednesbury irrationality: a decision is Wednesbury unreasonable if no reasonable authority properly directing itself on the relevant law and facts could have reached it: Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation [1948]. The ground for refusal — ideological opposition to supermarkets as a category — has nothing to do with the relevant planning considerations (impact on the locality, traffic, design, amenity). A planning authority must apply planning law criteria; refusing permission on the basis of a general political preference against large retailers falls outside the range of reasonable responses.

Illegality — irrelevant considerations: planning decisions must be made on relevant planning grounds. Disapproval of retail chains is not a material planning consideration under the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 — it is an irrelevant consideration that has been taken into account, rendering the decision unlawful.

Illegality — improper purpose: using planning powers to express ideological opposition to a business sector rather than to regulate land use is an improper purpose: Padfield principles.

Remedy: the developer should seek a quashing order to quash the refusal, and a mandatory order requiring the authority to reconsider the application on relevant planning grounds.

⚠️ Irrationality and illegality often overlap — a decision infected by irrelevant considerations is frequently also irrational.
Irrationality.summary_line: WEDNESBURY · IRRATIONALITY · IRRELEVANT CONSIDERATIONS · PLANNING · PADFIELD · IMPROPER PURPOSE · QUASH

Irrationality.front: The Home Secretary has a statutory power to grant or refuse leave to remain to asylum seekers.
